posts.author: ffrostfall
posts.content: This actually showcases a flaw with BridgeNet I’ll get on fixing when available. As of right now there’s no way to tell when BridgeNet is started . This means you’re kinda forced to use single-script architecture which is something I encourage you to research, but I’m not going to assume everyone who uses BridgeNet will do this. So, I think what I’m going to do is make functions such as CreateBridge yield until BridgeNet starts. However , it’s possible that it plays nicely without starting because starting BridgeNet connects to heartbeat and all that. I’m still going to make this functions yield until start because I don’t want completely unexpected behavior in my module the code snippet you asked for: Client local Object = BridgeNet.CreateBridge("Test") Object:Connect(function(arg1, arg2, arg3) print(arg1) end) while task.wait(1) do Object:Fire("Hello", "world") end Server local Object = BridgeNet.CreateBridge("Test") Object:Connect(function(plr, arg1, arg2) print(plr, arg1) end) while task.wait(1) do Object:FireTo(game.Players:GetPlayers()[1], "Received: Fire") end To answer your second question, the bridges are just connected by string, and you need to call CreateBridge separately on the client and the server. However, if you need to index a bridge that’s already created, you can do .FromBridge(). As of right now , this will return nil if it doesn’t exist, which means I could probably add in a .WaitForBridge() function to make this process easier. commitblue: about the start command, i think we shouldnt use a table as an arg. because the table indexes for this module are case-sensitive and people might forget if the index name is this or this, so why not make it like this: bridgeNet.Start(60, 60) anyway im interested in this module, i always have wanted to optimize remote events so thanks! I think one thing I could do is use symbols instead of text indexes, so it would be like BridgeNet.Start({ [BridgeNet.DefaultReceive] = 60, [BridgeNet.DefaultSend] = 60, }) You’re right, plain text keys are easy to forget and get confused (it’s already happened!), but the reason I don’t want to use the order of arguments is because of how difficult it is to change. Say I want to remove the default send and receive options in a year, what happens then? Backwards compatibility breaks. And thank you! Every bit of support means a lot to me.

posts.author: commitblue
posts.content: ffrostfall: BridgeNet.Start({ [BridgeNet.DefaultReceive] = 60, [BridgeNet.DefaultSend] = 60, }) An enum thing is also fine. Thank you for this! will there ever be roblox-ts support too?

posts.author: anon14466931
posts.content: Thank You!! One more follow up question. Assuming I use 1 script architecture, I still need to use 1 server script and 1 client script. Do I need to call BridgeNet.Start both on Server and on Client, or just call it only on the server?

items.title: A complete guide ~ How exploits work & how to best prevent them

posts.author: Clear_View
posts.content: Over the summer i’ve taken up reverse engineering and strengthened my C skills. I tend to see a lot of misinformation in threads regarding exploits, and now knowing how they work I felt it was time to make everything clear and help developers in preventing them. Firstly to clarify: yes, I have reversed Roblox to write an exploit to mess around with their Lua to get a feel for how it works. I don’t plan on ever publicly releasing my exploit nor using it for cheating in actual games. I just wrote it simply for learning purposes. First: what types of exploits are there? There’s a lot of exploits that exist for Roblox. The most popular are script executors, since they allow you to do almost anything you want without restrictions. There’s 3 main types of ways that exploiters achieve script execution, but they’re not too important to helping you develop counter measures. Second: why doesn’t Roblox do more to prevent exploiters? After seeing their security measures, I know they implement as much as they can to prevent exploiters. The only reason there’s so much exploits is because of smart and dedicated people in the community who work together to bypass Roblox’s checks, such as louka, eternal, defcon, ect. Roblox doesn’t do more to prevent exploiters simply because they can’t without risking breaking a lot of games, or it would require sketchy UAC privelages, which might make people think that Roblox is a virus. The anti-exploit development mostly comes down to you, as the developer. You understand your game the best and therefore can prevent exploits the best on your game. To clarify some things before moving on: 1. It’s near impossible to detect an expliot by just injecting it. Roblox does do this, but it’s actually not the hardest to get around. Some exploits are however detectable upon injection, we’ll cover that in a bit. 2. Roblox actively tries to stop exploiters. That’s why theres updates every week, to basically shuffle important info that you’d need to get an exploit working. 3. Roblox can’t just check if an exploit’s window is open. It does check for certain windows, but most exploits will just randomize their window name. 4. You have to write your anti-exploit around detecting scripts instead of exploits themselves. 5. Filtering Enabled is not a one fixes all solution. All this does is prevent exploiters from replicated stuff to the server without revsere engineering anything. 6. Asset script stealing is near impossible to prevent. Remember: anything that the client uses can be seen by exploiters, so don’t be dumb about what you let them see. 7. Exploiters CANNOT see server script code. It’s not replicated to them, so you can hide a lot of check inside them. Section 2: different types of exploits There’s 2 main types of ways that exploiters can mess with your game. Firstly: client script executors. These are the most common. These are the ones that just run only on your client and have to find a way to actually exploit your game. Some examp

posts.author: brokenVectors
posts.content: Couldn’t the exploiter just delete the connection or something? Plus, they could also spam the ConsoleError remote and (possibly) crash the server. Correct me if I’m wrong. And also, changing service names is useless since they could just use GetService() .

posts.author: Clear_View
posts.content: Yes, they could use something like for i,v in pairs(getconnections(game:GetService"Script Context".Error)) do v.Disable() end , but most won’t so that’s why I recommend just burying it inside your largest client script. And secondly, old scripts tend to not use GetService and most people won’t bother checking first if the names are randomized, so they might execute some old script and error.

posts.author: Autterfly
posts.content: There are a few issues with your last example. Starting with the second line, spawn isn’t needed. PlayerAdded creates a new thread every time already. Next, busy loops are bad practice. A better alternative would be plr.CharacterAdded:Wait() . After that, you define lastPosition but never actually update it. This means the player will never be able to venture far from where they spawned, whether exploiting or not. while wait() is also bad practice. You should avoid using it in the condition because it’s not obvious right away why it works and it’s not its intended use. There are also potential issues with plr.Character.HumanoidRootPart erroring if at any point Character is made nil (which I believe can happen briefly during death). It’s also questionable with how you calculate distance traveled. Depending on the terrain, it’s possible for a player to move further away from where they last were last check. Think falling or sliding for example. I get that it’s not supposed to be a perfect solution but those are common enough to consider.

posts.author: AlmostInTime
posts.content: Clear_View: game:GetService("ScriptContext").Error:connect(function(message, stack, scriptFrom) game:GetService("ReplicatedStorage").ConsoleError:FireServer(message, scriptFrom:GetFullName()) end) Used this method on a game I was working on for a while fully knowing it could be bypassed at any given time – and it eventually was. This guide is somewhat misleading to the extent that it encourages developers to “intecerpt” exploits before / while they’re happening. My best advice would be to secure your remotes using sanity checks on the server to validate data the client is requesting.

posts.author: Cerulean7
posts.content: Clear_View: Remember that most people who’ll exploit your game are script kiddies who just paste stuff off of V3rmillion, and hardly know what they’re doing, so you’ll probably have an advantage. on top of this, chances are that the exploiter will be using a universal script instead of one talored to your game (if your game isn’t on the front page). this means that they won’t have any specific methods of bypassing your game’s anticheat. client side anticheats aren’t necessarily bad, you just shouldn’t ever rely on them for obvious reasons. they should never be a replacement for serverside anticheats / remote security. you’ll stop the exploiters that have no clue what they’re doing (a considerable portion, see the initial quote). If someone ever makes a script that deletes it, you lose nothing except an hour of your time.

posts.author: dimsolstice
posts.content: You told us how to do simple obfuscation of the Service names then told us how to counteract it; game:GetService("ServiceName") . The best way to prevent exploiters from targeting your game is writing a good server-sided anti-cheat… That’s really all there is to it.

posts.author: sjr04
posts.content: Clear_View: You can put this script in either a server or client script, it doesn’t really matter. But if you put it inside the client, be sure to destroy the script after it runs. game:GetService("Lighting").Name = tostring(math.random()) game:GetService("Workspace").Name = tostring(math.random()) game:GetService("ReplicatedStorage").Name = tostring(math.random()) game:GetService("Players").Name = tostring(math.random()) game:GetService("ReplicatedFirst").Name = tostring(math.random()) This will now randomize the names of commonly used services in exploit. However, this could easily break your entire game if your scripts aren’t correctly written. To fix this, you should use game:GetService(“ServiceName”) instead of game.ServiceName. Clear_View: It’s good practice too anyway An excellent reason to not change the name of services. This really isn’t a way of preventing exploits.

posts.author: GalaxyGourmet
posts.content: I don’t like how you make your anti exploit on the client even when you know you have to do it on the server. It doesn’t matter how hidden it is, someone will find it. It’s also horrible practice to shuffle the names of services, because it only makes it more inconvenient for you, and if you can just call GetService(), so can the exploiter.

posts.author: serverOptimist
posts.content: here, let me let you all in on a idea that most developers overlook and I, due to my job in a studio have not been able to code up myself. The reason exploiters are able to exploit the player’s character is because the character is all on the client. If you ever noticed when you yourself lag, your character is walking just fine, on your client but n for others, you’re lagging and for you, they are lagging. This right here is the very reason exploiters can exploit their character and for instance, teleport across the map. Now, what you’d ideally want to do if anti exploiting is so important to you and top priority. You’d recode the BACKEND of the player controller and have the server side know the position on the server and all the client ever really does is predict where the input in the server is and if that input is offset from the server, then the client automatically corrects itselfand if a player tried to alter this in any way, shape or form on the client, thanks to your server side, you can detect if that player might’ve altered this at all. This is a true fix to exploiting. Not exploit prevention, but it makes the player’s character unable to really move on the client, rather, predict where the client moved to begin with and if the server and client posiion is offset,then the client will “lag back in time” to where the server’s last known position was. Of course, this has one major drawback, which is the deal breaker for a lot of people. Say someone had 400 ping, the game would be literally unplayable cause you’lll be lagging back in tine constantly cause the client will constantly be offset from the server to where it’ll always try and correct itself to the server’s last knownposition and repeat. That’s a majo drawback so if you are looking to run a game where lag has a huge difference, you’ll need to choose which is more important. 100% play ability or a more secure player controller.

posts.author: anon2793720
posts.content: Not too sure how your post is much different to the Exploiting Explained post but nonetheless, I have a few things to say: Clear_View: After seeing their security measures, I know they implement as much as they can to prevent exploiters. This is an interesting discussion point already, as we truly need to define what is “as much as they can”. Roblox certainly have implemented quite a fair share of security checks, such as the memcheck, random hackflags within the regular client and even Luau has some security checks for stack checks and the like. However, even with all that, you can execute code fairly simply without needing to bypass the majority of Roblox’s security checks. Moving away from client-side security though, Roblox are fairly naive when it even comes to their client → server architecture. Roblox will accept a lot of information without any scrutiny or sanity checks for it so in that perspective, Roblox has a lot of room for improvement - and the Roblox client is no exception. Ultimately, it’s always going to be a cat and mouse game and a lot of Roblox’s security issues can be attributed to technical debt. Clear_View: Roblox actively tries to stop exploiters. That’s why theres updates every week, to basically shuffle important info that you’d need to get an exploit working. This “shuffle” you’re referring to doesn’t have a massive impact to exploits. It’s part of the Roblox client build process and a lot of functions and whatnot that exploits need to function can be found by scanning for byte signatures within the client. Roblox update on a weekly basis as part of the Agile development system. This is why they use fast flags and have regular meetings, it’s simply just part of the development life cycle which a lot of companies adopt in one way or another. Clear_View: These ones are much more rare, and typically only happen through developer stupididty. With regards to server-side exploits on Roblox, it’s unfair to say this is due to developer stupidity because even in the real world, exploits happen due to insecure dependencies, etc. This is why auditing your code is important, making sure to use open-source libraries and understanding what you’re working with. Roblox is an ideal place to share these types of malicious backdoors, as a lot of the developers on the platform are not familiar with real world development standards and processes - such as code review, etc. This doesn’t make a developer on Roblox “stupid” but simply inexperienced as they’ve not had the real world experience to develop their skills. Perhaps making a code review guide for Roblox would be more beneficial than this post? Clear_View: Now, let’s make a lot more scripts that exploiters use error! This entire section you’ve written doesn’t really cover a lot about making their scripts fail. You’ve even mentioned that this can be fixed by using GetService() which is good practice, you’re right. At the very most, it’s a very slight inconvenience to an exploiter. If yo

posts.author: GameAnnounce
posts.content: First and foremost, not to rain on your parade, but this is not really a “complete” guide. There are many nuances to exploiting and for that matter exploit detection and deterrence. Clear_View: First: what types of exploits are there? There’s a lot of exploits that exist for Roblox. The most popular are script executors, since they allow you to do almost anything you want without restrictions. There’s 3 main types of ways that exploiters achieve script execution, but they’re not too important to helping you develop counter measures. This section is redundant, there aren’t really different ways to be able to execute scripts on Roblox - there’s no compiler on the client. It’s as simple as generate some bytecode, feed it to the deserializer, and run. Clear_View: Second: why doesn’t Roblox do more to prevent exploiters? After seeing their security measures, I know they implement as much as they can to prevent exploiters. The only reason there’s so much exploits is because of smart and dedicated people in the community who work together to bypass Roblox’s checks, such as louka, eternal, defcon, ect. Roblox doesn’t do more to prevent exploiters simply because they can’t without risking breaking a lot of games, or it would require sketchy UAC privelages, which might make people think that Roblox is a virus. The anti-exploit development mostly comes down to you, as the developer. You understand your game the best and therefore can prevent exploits the best on your game. To clarify some things before moving on: It’s near impossible to detect an expliot by just injecting it. Roblox does do this, but it’s actually not the hardest to get around. Some exploits are however detectable upon injection, we’ll cover that in a bit. Roblox actively tries to stop exploiters. That’s why theres updates every week, to basically shuffle important info that you’d need to get an exploit working. Roblox can’t just check if an exploit’s window is open. It does check for certain windows, but most exploits will just randomize their window name. You have to write your anti-exploit around detecting scripts instead of exploits themselves. Filtering Enabled is not a one fixes all solution. All this does is prevent exploiters from replicated stuff to the server without revsere engineering anything. Asset script stealing is near impossible to prevent. Remember: anything that the client uses can be seen by exploiters, so don’t be dumb about what you let them see. Exploiters CANNOT see server script code. It’s not replicated to them, so you can hide a lot of check inside them. This section is almost entirely opinion and speculation, but incorrect speculation at that. The battle of developer v exploiter is one entirely of catch-up. Preventative measures will always be circumvented: where there’s a will there’s a way. I don’t agree that Roblox doesn’t do more because they can’t due to “risk of breakage”, but instead that it’s inherently difficult to do anything to prevent or detect explo

posts.author: Officiall_Studios
posts.content: Correct me if I’m wrong, but can’t you do :FireServer(“legitEvent”) and then do in the handler, :Connect(function(Event) if Event == “legitEvent” then —Do stuff Else return end? Sorry I’m on mobile so I can’t do the lua symbols.

posts.author: brokenVectors
posts.content: The exploiter could easily use something like RemoteSpy and figure out that they just have to fire the event with “legitEvent”. You shouldn’t try to use any of those weird key techniques, just don’t trust the client since the end user can do pretty much anything.

posts.author: Blaroo
posts.content: Correct me if I’m wrong, What can Exploiters do Exploiters can disable functions in a Client side script, For example Player:Kick() You can’t kick them from Client-Side, even if you fire a remote to the server to kick the exploiter, it won’t work They could do simple stuff like Walkspeed and JumpPower, they could also Fly Also they could Decompile Client scripts and checks how your system works on client and how to abuse remotes Try not to include important stuff in a Client side script Don’t make Client sided Anti Cheats because its pretty much useless and exploiters can delete them or bypass it Remember Exploiters can control anything related to Client Side so be careful. NetworkOwnership Recently exploiters can do custom animations because of SimulationRadius They can do other stuff as well, they could bring players with Tools and Network. They can control Not Anchored Parts They can control Hats with network They can make objects with hat parts ( Delete meshes from the hats ) MaximumSimulationRadius has been patched by Roblox. SimulationRadius Patch: NetworkOwnership Exploit Patch Backdoors They mostly come from free Models Exploiters can launch a backdoor check, if you have a backdoor in-game, the exploiter has Server side access, So please be careful with free models. You could easily prevent most Backdoor checks by inserting a remote and if the Exploiter fires it, it kicks him or logs the Exploiter or whatever you want to do with the Exploiter, this won’t be a lot useful. Securing your remotes Don’t do checks on Client-side, for Example a Buy button, instead of checking from Client-side just fire the remote and let the server check the currency that the Player has since Exploiters can manipulate Client-side. if your remotes aren’t secure, Exploiters will 100% abuse it

posts.author: ForbiddenJ
posts.content: There is a lot of truth some of what you said. The fundamental client-server model of Roblox gaming is a bit of a mess, with various compromises made to keep the game playable, but which also leaves open doors for cheating. There indeed is an entire community dedicated to cheating, and the manpower behind cheat development is huge, like so huge that client-side anticheats are only good on a rolling basis. There is also a lot of scripted models created and botted to the top with unscrupulous intent. These models use heavy obfuscation to hide their true intent and try to seem innocent by containing comments along the lines of “obfuscated to prevent stealing” or “this script is required by Roblox Studio. Do not delete.” However, I have some criticisms. Clear_View: Backdoors are usually hidden away far inside some script, so this is why you should check over every script a free model uses if you’re using FMs. Exploiters often try to conceal their backdoors behind obfuscated code, but i’ve never seen one be actually smart about it. An easy way to tell if something is backdoored is if you see obfuscated code, or just Ctrl + F and search for “require” and it’s hidden away. Many of the front-page backdoored items go as far as hiding the keyword itself behind more obfuscation so you don’t find it with Ctrl + F, usually through a combination of getfenv and insane string manipulation. Here’s a simple example: local a = getfenv a()[string.reverse("eriuqer")](12345) Clear_View: We’re going to randomize the names of a lot of DataModel children that scripts use. This will require a bit of work to write your game’s scripts with, but it’s worth it. (i promise) I don’t like being verbose with GetService throughout my game code. For publicly-shared scripts, yes, anything can happen to service names, which is why it’s good practice, but for game code written for its respective game, I’m historically more productive using game.ServiceName whenever it’s available. Autocomplete works better with that. Coupled with the fact that many exploits, whether game-specific or in-general, account for randomized service names, it doesn’t seem worth the trade-off, at least to me. Also, take note that randomizing Workspace is useless since there is a hard-coded property such that game.Workspace will work regardless. Clear_View: If you have a money remote, did the player award themself an insane amount of money? What if the cheater fired the remote with normal amounts a million times, instead of once with an insane amount? Okay, let’s use a rate limiter ! Then what if the cheater is firing the remote when they are in a location where they shouldn’t be awarded money at all? Okay let’s use check zones in the game world! Then what if a cheater briefly teleports into a valid zone and then fires it before your anti-teleport example code gets around to detecting them. The point is, it would be better that only server scripts can award money. There should be no remotes for awarding yoursel

posts.author: takticiadam
posts.content: Blaroo: even if you fire a remote to the server to kick the exploiter, it won’t work It will work. the kick on the server has to work because it cant be tampered with.

posts.author: takticiadam
posts.content: Blaroo: MaximumSimulationRadius has been patched by Roblox. exploits have bypassed this now, thanks to this now they also have figured out to get your country and osPlatform

posts.author: Autterfly
posts.content: What he means is that firing a remote event can be intercepted by the client. So if you attempt to kick via remote it can be disabled from going out too.

posts.author: formy2kids
posts.content: I really don’t get the point of this post. The main server-sided checks you should have to prevent exploiting is anti-teleport (checking position), anti-speed (checking velocity), and anti-noclip (raycasts/:GetTouchingParts()). Renaming services is a waste of time. The only thing you should worry about regarding client-sided anti-cheat is a quick LocalScript that detects changes in the Humanoid (to prevent the script kiddies). Remotes are easy to protect. Just make sure that the player is actually allowed to fire it using some if then checks. This isn’t really a “complete guide” if the only server-sided check covered is position checks. You also need to add a lot of checks to your script to make sure it doesn’t error and let exploiters roam freely. You have to check to make sure the character exists before it is referenced, and then make sure the HumanoidRootPart exists using if :FindFirstChild().

items.title: Server Authority Studio Beta - Insights from Engineers!

posts.author: Khanovich
posts.content: TLDR: This post will evolve a bit over time This is a minimum set of initial APIs that will be expanded upon. Even smaller than Early Access We will expand the APIs during the lifetime of the Studio beta Always easier to expand APIs (instead of reducing) without negatively impacting Roblox Creators Default Avatars should just work, and easily customizable Functionality is focused around scripts that have control loops that are bound to RunService::BindToSimulation Currently the system works best with simple, one-assembly mechanisms Complex mechanisms (cars with suspensions, ETC) still work with the API but experience quality will most likely be reduced There is quite a bit of work on our part to improve this Some notes on two required features to enable Server Authority Deeper dive on some of the Known Issues Hi everyone! Thanks for being interested in the Server Authority beta, this is something that many of us have dreamed of having on the platform for many years, and being so close to a first release has us extremely excited! We wanted to take a moment to set up some expectations for the initial Server Authority Studio Beta. Some of you may know that once an API is in Roblox, it’s in Roblox forever-ish. Because of this, the current beta is the MINIMUM set of APIs that we feel confident about. This does mean that this is not a complete and final API. We’ve spent a lot of time removing one off hacks that enabled the Early Access Beta APIs, throughout the Roblox engine, allowing us to ship the Server Authority Studio Beta built upon a sustainable core tech stack. This initial API unlocks a lot of Server Authoritative content, so we want to get it in Creators` hands ASAP for further testing and feedback! We expect that as you start developing some content you will run into pain points and will want to request API extensions. This is part of the process and we want your feedback so that we can methodically extend the capabilities of Server Authority before the fully playable launch in early 2026. We have internally verified the existing API by writing movement abilities for player characters, simple sports games, racing experiences, and shooting games, but these are going to be much simpler experiences than anything you will build, which is why the feedback will be key. The most effective way to request new features is to describe your use-case in detail! Another callout is that we have been mostly focused on feature completeness, so expect performance to significantly improve before the initial launch for Server and Clients. The current Server Authority technology has been built and optimized primarily for simple mechanisms, like the player characters/avatars. When opted into the Server Authority Studio Beta, if you open a baseplate and change Workspace.AuthorityMode to Server (There may be some pieces of core tech you may need to enable first) and hit play, you will be playing on Server Authoritative default character avatar without writing any c

posts.author: spelled_ayayron
posts.content: Hi! Thanks for this resource. I had a quick question about cloning instances during simulation. With projectiles, I’m unable to clone parts during the simulation callback or use other RunService events. How might I create projectiles at runtime such that they’re visible for everyone properly to simulate bullets/bullet tracers? EDIT: Nevermind! I saw that Laser Tag was updated for Server Authority. I’ll be checking that out instead. Sorry!

posts.author: TenebrisNoctua
posts.content: Khanovich: The current Server Authority technology has been built and optimized primarily for simple mechanisms, like the player characters/avatars. When opted into the Server Authority Studio Beta, if you open a baseplate and change Workspace.AuthorityMode to Server (There may be some pieces of core tech you may need to enable first) and hit play, you will be playing on Server Authoritative default character avatar without writing any code. “It just works( )”. The PlayerModule doesn’t have any methods to stop the movement code for a specific player however, I think that should be improved upon. Khanovich: RunService::setPredictionMode This seems to be referring a C++ method that cannot be accessed from Luau. Khanovich: We are missing a “RunService::OnRollback” event that lets creators reset custom state when mispredictions must be corrected I believe we had a .Misprediction event before in RunService ? Were they removed due to :BindToSimulation() ? It would be really useful if we had events that allowed us to detect when certain things happen under Server Authority. Attribute limits are understandable, but a solution (such as dividing the data?) must be found because a lot of people use them. And you kind of need attributes to have rollback support for certain values. I’m personally thinking of creating an AttributeManager that automatically creates multiple Instances for a certain Instance’s Attribute so we can achieve more data.

posts.author: MrMoled
posts.content: Thank you for the transparency! Could you expand on the reasons for the reliance on attributes for game state? Using many attributes will drastically increase the amount of data other users send and receive, which may not be useful. Such as if there’s an ammo count that will need to be predicted and possibly rolled back, but only on one client. Using attributes will still end up replicating that data to users who don’t need that data. I understand that it’s probably the simplest route to managing state, but I’d appreciate it if we were given more control over attributes themselves or a separate API that allows Luau variables to be rolled back programmatically.

posts.author: EmeraldSlash
posts.content: Khanovich: There are no more ordering guarantees for property updates! This breaks the guarantee that when a RemoteEvent arrives that all properties that were updated BEFORE the event was sent would have replicated. Doesn’t this break like.. everything? What are the boundaries of it? (Is it only affecting things in Workspace etc.) Are there any plans to add replication fences or something like that which enable replication to be forcibly ordered/grouped?

posts.author: Khanovich
posts.content: Rio: Doesn’t this break like.. everything? What are the boundaries of it? (Is it only affecting things in Workspace etc.) It breaks anything that assumes this kind of Replication Fencing behavior, which is why the architecture is behind a Workspace flag instead of being enabled for all experiences. Are there any plans to add replication fences or something like that which enable replication to be forcibly ordered/grouped? Yeah we want to add “Local Remote Events” that are synchronous with some instance. But in general, global “replication fencing” is something we internally consider bad because it makes it impossible to make the engine scale better.

posts.author: EmeraldSlash
posts.content: Khanovich: It breaks anything that assumes this kind of Replication Fencing behavior, which is why the architecture is behind a Workspace flag instead of being enabled for all experiences. Yes, but for example there are various other systems that may be outside of Workspace but which are still replicated which this could impact I believe. For example, replicating instances via PlayerGui, or via ReplicatedStorage. Are those affected by these changes as well? Or is it just for things in Workspace.

posts.author: Khanovich
posts.content: Yeah those are also affected by that setting.

posts.author: EmeraldSlash
posts.content: Khanovich: Yeah we want to add “Local Remote Events” that are synchronous with some instance. But in general, global “replication fencing” is something we internally consider bad because it makes it impossible to make the engine scale better. Cool. Although it sounds like you’re thinking about an API where you have to create a separate remote event for every instance? For what my opinion is worth, that seems bothersome, I would prefer an API like this: local RemoteEvent = Instance.new("RemoteEvent", game.ReplicatedStorage) for _, Instance in Instances do RemoteEvent:FireClientSynced(Player, Instance, ...) end where you just do all the caching/instance setup under the hood, instead of making us do it with: for _, Instance in Instances do local LocalRemoteEvent = Instance.new("LocalRemoteEvent", Instance) LocalRemoteEvent:FireClient(Player, ...) end

posts.author: index_self
posts.content: Synced remote firing is actually a really good idea, especially with streaming involved. If you could be able to send an instance reference over but have the engine queue it until the client has fully replicated everything by that point, then you wouldn’t have to worry about streamed model references coming back as nil.

posts.author: XenoDenissboss1
posts.content: I appreciate these sorts of posts. Honestly i would really like if we got more in depth explanations of other engine changes and updates like this. The post below is a rather long read written at 4 am so i apologize. But hopefully it can maybe push some change here. Khanovich: Workspace.StreamingEnabled NextGenerationReplication does a lot of extra CPU work on the Server that didn’t exist before, since we are prioritizing all Instance property updates (something we didn’t have to do with FIFO logic before). Streaming provinces a necessary scalability limit on how much prioritization work needs to happen on the Server. It’s also a key requirement for how we plan to make Server Authority work on low-end devices. Additionally, RunService::setPredictionMode relies on atomicity/instance grouping that only exists in Streaming. (note: this may not be fully enabled on the first beta version) There are other core functionalities from streaming that we depend on under the hood, and not all of them are currently enabled. Look yeah, i get it that you cant really predict over a thousand parts with tons of properties at once. I get that the replication system has been juiced up and needs some optimization on the side to save on performance. But requiring streaming enabled for this is just absurd to me still considering this is mostly used, as youve said here, just as an instance manager to check what to prioritize. Maybe it makes sense to you, your team and whoever else. But it does not make sense to me and plenty other developers who have had to work (and suffer) with streaming enabled. I dont understand this culture at roblox in which GOOD features are being forcefully paired with other features. Sure, i get it to some degree. It’s technically easier to work with X Y and Z if theire being tied to whatever else but this only serves to HURT many other developers looking for higher levels of granularity for their own projects. One big example that really pissed me off is the introduction of the interior environment reflection maps. Amazing feature though and through except oh wait, its entirely automatic, unpredictable and it’s tied to one specific lighting property that just so happens to also change how the entire shading of your experience looks like. Also with the excuse that manual controls wont be provided because: Indoor Dynamic Environment Maps Beta Roblox highly believes that instances used in the engine should represent the real world. There is no such thing as a “lighting probe” in the physical world, so we cannot add such concepts. Personally? I believe this is a full on insult to the developers working on this platform. But whatever, this post isnt about cube map reflections. You specifically mention the next generation replication performing a lot of extra work on the CPU. I have to ask… how much more exactly? Are there some numbers we can view? Is there some sort of percentage? How much harder is it to replicate one singular instance now? We have

posts.author: Khanovich
posts.content: XenoDenissboss1: (Dont even get me started on the “just working” creation engine and starfield man . Quite ironic.). The irony was intended, because this is a Beta and my theory on how things will “just work” are surely going to explode in my face once more creators are testing the exsting functionalities and poke holes in what we’ve shipped. on Streaming + Server Authority. Look, I get it. Every game developer/creator on Roblox knows what their content needs out of the engine better than any individual Roblox engineer or even Roblox executive does. That`s just the nature of how big the platform is and how many creators there are. Roblox is always stuck with the hard job of figuring out how we can deliver the best tech for the most amount of people. This might mean that sometimes when we ship something, it’s not immediately the best thing for everyone in the ecosystem, but we hope it’s the best for the most amount of people as possible. Server Authority has been an insanely complex project, considering how long of a legacy Roblox has with Client Authoritative player physics. I’m not gonna pretend that there isn’t a world/alternate reality where we toss even more engineers and QA at the issue and create a Server Authority that works when Next Generation Replication is disabled, or if DeferredSignals are disables, or if Streaming is disabled… but my experience with truly difficult projects like this is that tossing more engineers and more people at the problem rarely creates a good cohesive final product and results in shipping something mediocre with lower quality. We’re still internally hyper focusing on A LOT of areas that are fundamental requirements to make Server Authority an actually good product for the Platform. Things like: Resimulation Performance on Low End Devices Server Performance Mispredictions on Simple and Complex Interactions (As you posted in the other thread, with running on a Ball not working by default) Fixing the remaining “Client Authoritative Legacy Logic” (don’t get me started on figuring out how to make Tool Welds server Authoritative) Streaming team has built and optimized a lot of really complex systems that are not apparent to creators/developers under the hood because the only way they experience them is via things like Atomic Streaming . But internally these systems make a ton of internal calculations to group instances into higher level “Sets” that make it much easier for us to prioritize and group replicated data. They will also help us identify which instances need to be predicted when a player interacts with something physically. Without these systems Server Authority will just be a big bag of APIs that barely work unless you spend two years reading a manual and understanding Roblox Networking Architecture. On an orthogonal level, a chief concern of ours is making Server Authority work for low-end devices that can barely simulate their own avatar, much less 200ms of resimulation every time they mispredict. Havin

posts.author: BonkuraMan
posts.content: Thank you for the detailed communication. I think many power developers would still prefer access to more APIs, while also providing the existing ones as-is for most developers. I would not mind having a lot of caveats and less support for say, more precise control out of stream in/out. As it is now we do need to pull and reimplement a lot ourselves anyway- and that means trying to hack the guard rails into moving aside anyway. Which does itself often create spurious bugs.

posts.author: Khanovich
posts.content: XenoDenissboss1: So again i have to ask, how come roblox isnt investing in a new engine? This is a whole can of worms I’m not really prepared to talk about at length, because the topics you touched on are at the scale of “How to run Roblox as a business”, and I spent my 12 years working here primarily focused on the Game Engine and it’s capabilities. So I can riff on some thoughts that are my own, but don’t take this as me speaking for the company. Roblox is constantly evolving and improving the engine, this is pretty easy to see if you look at what it looked like 10, 5, 2 years ago. We’re constantly refactoring things under the hood for performance, new features, and a lot work on architectural sustainability. Any good engineer probably should ask themselves “should I just re-write this?” as a way to re-validate decisions or simply for self reflection on their prior work. A New Engine has an upside of being able to evaluate legacy architectural decisions more easily, but at the cost a lot of engineering resources and significant potential risks for the thousands of small businesses that already have content on Roblox today (whether this is because LTS support isn’t enough for what they need, or whether they now need to migrate to Roblox 2.0…). But right now im starting to see more and more engineers throwing the finger at the engine itself for X Y and Z. I hope you didn’t interpret my explanation of the systems we use for Server Authority from Streaming as me “blaming the engine”. Every Game Engine, and every complex piece of technology is going to have it’s caveats. Good software engineering systems generally have an architecture that defines some base level rules for how the system should be extended and what capabilities it should be able to add in the future and when developers make changes they have to consider that architecture, which comes with constraints. You then have two choices, expand the architecture, or just hack some garbage on top of it ignoring the architecture… but you get the feature you want! Most real software that people actually use is a weird mixture of both of these. The more mature software is probably a much larger portion of proper architecture, with some hacks, while the least mature software is mostly hacks and little bit of architecture. The only Software I can imagine being perfectly architected is software described by Academic Papers, and that’s because it’s easy to make things perfect “in theory”. So, in connection to my comment: Fixing the remaining “Client Authoritative Legacy Logic” (don’t get me started on figuring out how to make Tool Welds server Authoritative) Even if we built a brand new game engine from scratch, it’s not like all of a sudden figuring out how to support Tools and Equipment on Roblox 2.0 would go away, right? All it would mean is that we wouldn’t be able to just use the old client-authoritative logic, we would have to re-implement an equipment system from scratch. And we would still hav

posts.author: TenebrisNoctua
posts.content: Khanovich: Generally engineers don’t make posts like this because explaining the logic behind some of our decision making is kind of like leaving yourself vulnerable for attack. By giving you details on how we made these decisions, it’s technically more ammo for you if you wanted to nit-pick those decisions and double down. If you think about it, there really is no benefit to the company/engineer to do it since it’s virtually impossible to truly communicate the lifetime of learnings that led to these decisions in a blog-post style. But my hope is that it at least puts a somewhat human face and explains the rationale, even if you don’t necessarily agree with some of the technical decisions. I heavily agree with this part. This applies to almost every software there is today, you cannot understand the decision making completely without being involved in the creation of the software. Outside criticism is great, and allows you to achieve perhaps a better system and architecture, but it is still outside. You have to look at the system from both in and out in order to make a good decision.

posts.content: This might be stupid to ask, but can RunService:BindToSimulation also fire more than once on the same frame on the server ? I know it runs multiple times on the client when there’s a mispredict, but does it also try to ‘catch up’ if the server’s ‘framerate’ is too slow? If this is the case, then doing something like spawning a deferred task to destroy an object would be unsafe, no? items.title: My Problem with SSA frameworks (notably Knit)

posts.author: lukethebestpug
posts.content: Heyo, (why can’t i indent) So recently, I began work on a game, and it was half-way done. Then, I noticed that the code didn’t look pretty, like for my character state machine I had a singular function just for vaulting with a bunch of hardcoded values etc. and this (to me) looked ugly. I needed to apply body gyros and velocities to the character in two seperate instances:(the dictionary definition not roblox) where the character had to jump from a bar to another, and one for vaulting over objects. I turned it into a function because I also thought it was just hard to look at. I hated it. I hated it so much and I had no solution so after my game is half done I decided to scrap it in favour of a rewrite. Why did I do this? Because (in my head) I thought that; it was ugly it was unprofessional it was like spaghetti and i felt like i couldn’t add on to it without a headache wondering where to do something I needed some way to organize my game, so I tried looking for frameworks. Now I never used a framework before because they were so complicated, and I never really knew what they were used for because the language used by their creators is so technical and hard to read. Anyways… I tried using Knit. I made some dumb state machine thing that isn’t really a state machine because all it does is change a string value on function. At this point I really felt like giving up because frameworks in general are just complicated. Sleitnick’s only appliable tutorial on using Knit in a game never really covers things that SSA frameworks are useful for; modularity and breaking down complex mechanics in a readable and editable fashion. Not only this, he constantly replaces modules and changes the API to an unreadable mess. There used to be a function in his Component module called “.Auto(folder: folder)” and it automatically loaded all components in a folder (duh). The API included a mention of how using .Auto() is the best practice for loading components. Now, that mechanic and information is missing from the API, it doesn’t explain the best way to do it, and people had to ask about it themselves to others just to figure it out. Programming in this modular way has always given me a headache. It adds so much unnecessary complication to the process of simply “Starting A Project” that it makes me not want to start. I genuinely feel lost when trying to program with this format. I know it’s possible, because a lot of games use Knit, but I really don’t feel like it’s for me. I feel obligated to use a framework or an SSA structure because all the “iNdUstRy pRoFeSsIonAls” say it’s the best practice. I’m not saying using modules is bad, but using modules for every single thing in your game feels intoxicatingly complex for something that doesn’t even need to be. I can’t, I literally just can’t. I’m turned away by MSA because of my previous experiences of not being able to handle complex scripts with specific actions like my previous example, but then I’m also unable to flue

posts.author: msix29
posts.content: Hey, it’s great that you care about organization, like seriously, I see people just throwing scripts here and there and calling it a day. NOTE: I have no idea what SSA and MSA even mean, but I grasped the meaning from the contexts in which they were used MSA (aka, normal coding?) I liked to structure things in this way (same way for the server): client (local script) |— Module A |— Inner module A |— Module B |— Module C And you get it, the main client file loads these modules in order, I like making each module do a specific feature for the game. Now for the pros and the cons. Pros Modularity You can easily expose the functions you want to expose and hide others - no need to create different files. You mostly won’t need BindableEvents and such. You can load the files in the order YOU want That also allows u to comment out the loading of a module, effectively stopping that feature! Cons You said you didn’t like it lol, but this is here, just in case you used another method and maybe you like this one more. SSA (Knit) I would take a wild guess and say, you’re issue is that you tried writing code in the framework, not writing code for the framework [spoiler](Did I say that right?)[/spoiler]. Yup that’s it, I’m done. Jokes aside, you said you used components, for example, that’s quite the usual, but before Knit, did you ever use such component? No? Then you used classes, so continue to do it! You don’t have to change your entire codebase to be perfect for the framework, where perfect is how others use the framework. It’s your own game, you define that “perfect”, general idea is, controllers and services, that’s it, everything else is all up to you. I personally like having things like this: image 140×150 3.36 KB Classes, well, contains classes lol Controllers, I don’t think I should’ve mentioned those 2 but ykyk FixedModules, aka constants, but constants didn’t work pretty well as they are more of pre-runtime configuration, meaning they NEVER get changed during runtime and don’t expose any functions. Modules, these are ones that get changed during runtime, have no config (all in FixedModules !) and expose functions. The init.client.lua is the “loader”, you probably know of that by now, it’s simply: local ReplicatedStorage = game:GetService("ReplicatedStorage") local Knit = require(ReplicatedStorage.Packages.Knit) Knit.AddControllersDeep(script.Controllers) Knit.Start({ServicePromises = false}):andThen(function() print("Knit Client Started") end):catch(warn) Now for pros and cons, again! Pros Very organized Easy networking Idk, I can’t think of stuff on the spot man Cons Farewell, intellisense. You completely lose it, well except if you’re crazy like me and edited a whole damn LSP just to add knit support Pre ending, almost there! pugen: (why can’t i indent) You are writing with markdown , you can’t indent, except with black magic! Use: ensp; or nbsp; Yup, manually put these monstrosities! Don’t even question it. Ending I know you’ve been waiting for

posts.author: lukethebestpug
posts.content: Sorry, MSA - Multi-Script-Architecture Basically means using more than one server or client script to make your game function, etc. It’s what most people use and what you probably imagine when you think of programming, use of modules here and there but mostly for utility and making processes easier. SSA - Single-Script-Architecture This is where there’s one script on the client and one script on the server and they initialize a framework and/or load modules. From this point when modules are loaded the source code of the game is stored in the modules, and everything is executed in the modules, you can probably imagine how that could be confusing or complex on it’s own.

posts.author: lukethebestpug
posts.content: By the way, for your “MSA” example, is this one part of your game or the entire game? Because in that sense it would also be considered SSA, just ordered differently. I don’t hate Knit I just don’t want to use, Knit is just the only framework I’ve actually tried. Programming using SSA and module loaders just seems extra complex. Like for my state machine I really just don’t understand how to handle it at all.

posts.author: msix29
posts.content: MSA is what I used to do in my very very veeeeeryyyyyyy early days lol, I liked SSA and module loading more, also since I love (and use, quite a lot) Rust, it was easier to get used to writing Rust when my brain was already into SSA, and now here I am using it for everything.

posts.author: msix29
posts.content: It’s more MSA, you will have 1 like that for the server, which is really SSA, but for the client you usually have one for UI, sounds, effects, StarterPlayerScripts , StarterCharacterScripts , etc. (or whichever ones you need), and also when a UI is complex, instead of doing that for the whole UI I break it down so complex ones can be handled alone, although I never had issues with 1 main loader only. So I would guess this is kinda between SSA and MSA. That kinda proves my point of being too much into SSA, lmao. It’s way better than MSA in general, personal preference and opinion, of course.

posts.author: lukethebestpug
posts.content: Yea, I understand it has great advantages and whatnot. One of the biggest problems I’ve had is just having zero reference information of how people genuinely build games using SSA. I really want to try making this all work out the problem is there’s none really making any sort of complex tutorials on how frameworks are used. If I had cancer, my make-a-wish would be to see all of the modules in some reasonably complex game that uses Knit, lol. It’s cool and all in theory seeing it in videos but like it’s so hard to apply the bare-bones skills taught with the videos to a real scenario like I said with the state machine.

posts.author: msix29
posts.content: I feel you, even I after watching those videos had basically no idea what to do. They aren’t descriptive, at all. I learned the idea from the documentation and followed my own path in organization. For your cancer wish , I can show you some of my projects if you want, with and without Knit.

posts.author: apenzijncoolenleuk1
posts.content: If you’re working alone, don’t worry so much about pretty code. As long as you can make sense of it, it’s okay. Try using comments as much as possible. Comments are the most important thing when programming. Your code will work without comments -sure- but you won’t be able to adjust it in a reasonable amount of time. Use a consistent coding style, and try to keep everything formatted nicely. You don’t need a framework. If you don’t enjoy working with them, don’t use them. It’s better to make progress than to fight against a framework.

posts.author: WreckDough
posts.content: I would say that frameworks add overhead and complexity, but in the long run help massively. Its not even an organization thing, its a standardization thing. Most people can work short-term in an ‘unorganized’ environment and be just fine because they remember how everything works. But you’ll regret coming back to a codebase a year later and see a mess of different styles and setups. The other thing frameworks can do is speed up development because theres more features immediately available, and you can more quickly leverage previously written code. However the benefits of such only come after working with frameworks for a while and getting comfortable with them.

posts.author: lukethebestpug
posts.content: truth be told, organizing is really good. if you are going to use SSA (i highly recommend) and you just want to make a game, I suggest not using a framework. But you MUST use VSCode (in my opinion), it is insanely helpful for literally everything. If you are hellbent on using Knit, go ahead I can’t stop you. Make sure you at least TRY to format your code, keep a consistent style, probably use correct casings, etc. And lastly, practice. Practice practice practice. One of the best ways to get accustomed to using SSA architecture and VSCode is to just practice making random junk. If it’s simple, repeat it until it’s absolutely perfect every time, and you’re fast at it. Or if it’s advanced go hog wild and refactor later. It’s all about learning. so go do it!!! thanks for everyone’s help and i hope others find this useful in the future

posts.author: 3rdhoan123
posts.content: I can’t really say much since I don’t know exactly what optimizations you’re preforming so I’ll just give a general consensus on what I remember: Basic animation optimization Culling on the animation/characters (if not in view, stop focusing energy/performance on them) Optimizing mesh/parts of the character, make further npcs less detailed/use less performance heavy meshes Basic collision optimizations, with enough parts, even the most basic of parts can become laggy when multiple collisions occur. Shadow performance, often overlooked but if the characters are far enough, disable shadows on them Don’t know how you handle movement but try to make the movement part “client” based with the server handling verification checks (checking where the npc should be). Client = effects, server = verification

posts.author: TheCraneStyle
posts.content: So, you have to give up on direct position syncing and instead use direction and mag vectors only. And only change or update them if they are changed in some way. The idea is you can simulate more and have to send less data per frame because the only timw the client will be informes is when the vector has changed. Otherwise the client can simulate the position of the character and since the server is emulating this tye clients simulation will be very close. Again this is an over simplification, update rate limiting by distance, culling by whats in view plays a large roll ontop of ensuring your update data is as small and clean as possible. Also, if these npcs dont need to ve synced and are all managed client side, hutting that number is not unfathomable.

posts.author: Andolino13
posts.content: You could also make a CollissionGroup for all NPCs and set their own CollissionGroup to CanCollide false. This prevents the collissions between NPCs which should reduce lag aswell. Except you want that NPCs can collide each other.

If you wish you could even give a few one target and a few a different target. The NPCs would just move along the path. Would have to play around with it a bit to see how much you can take away from the NPCs script and do in the master script.

posts.author: Veexas
posts.content: The server handles the npc information such as position in a table, use that position to pathfind and u will get array of pathfinding points, use those points in order by putting task.wait(amount of time it will take before the npc reach next point by calculating the magnitude between 2 points divided by the npc walkspeed) then update the npc position in table into the next point, and the client have local script that creates npc and position them with the position the server is telling u for each npc, By doing this, u save the server from having to calculate the physics of each npc, so it gets way faster, also u can put the npc character into replicated storage if they are too far from ur player, so ur device wont need to render that, but the position still gets updated whenever u receive new data from the server

items.title: Simulating thousands of moving NPCs with humanoids/physics performantly

posts.author: me7474
posts.content: Table of Contents Introduction Getting started New enemy function bitpacking writing a bitpacker Networking architecture (server) Networking architecture (client) Client rendering Server handling Showcase Closing thoughts the table of contents does not work right now, 0 clue what I did wrong. I am tired and will fix it in the morning. Introduction Around a year ago I was looking for a way to simulate large amounts of NPCs without significant server/client lag or latency due to high network usage. For my purposes these NPCs needed to maintain their humanoids so they could interact with unanchored parts using the built in physics system. I eventually arrived at an implementation that took me from 30 NPCs causing me significant server lag (running default humanoids on the server) to being able to run 500 NPCs with an average of ~46 kb/s received networking data on client, or 1000 NPCs with ~112 kb/s. At these numbers the greatest constraint is the device the client is playing on, I found anything not high end struggled far before 500 due to all my NPCs moving and being animated. This tutorial will be kept somewhat non specific, I will be covering architecture and networking in detail but to truly achieve whatever you want to do with your NPCs you will likely need some sort of state machine or behavior controller giving your NPCs actual logic. There is ample support for this should you wish to implement it however it is outside the scope of what I wish to cover (perhaps in a part two ). Special shoutout to @Devalvion and his Calculon series for inspiring me to finally write up a post. Highly recommend his work, very well thought out and technically impressive topics being explained. Getting started In order to fully optimize your NPCs you need to have a Server NPC and a Client NPC . The server NPC is a barebones hitbox that solely exists to simulate physics pictured below: image 726×370 38.5 KB and the client NPC is the visual aspect of the system: image 754×498 72.7 KB You’ll want to make the server NPC the dimensions of the physics hitbox you want for your NPC. In my case you will see it is the same height as the Noob, however it is only two studs wide as I did not want the NPC to have arm collisions. Place these rigs wherever you want, but make sure the client NPC is somewhere the client can access. In my case I put them both in replicated storage. New enemy function The architecture I used requires a server script, local script, and module script. You also need a remote event to send enemy data to clients. I placed this remote along with the module script in replicated storage, the server script in server script service, and the local script in starter player scripts. Finally I created a folder called “Enemies” located under workspace. Let’s go over how this system actually works before we make it. When an NPC is created we spawn the server rig on the server and crucially don’t replicate it. For most applications the majority of built in humanoid

posts.author: me7474
posts.content: what have you done clientside to optimize? I feel I am much better at networking optimization than I am rendering and have always found FPS to be the limiting factor lol. My most recent version of this system I am able to get 1,750 NPCs at ~60 FPS with ~100 kb/s receive (notable spikes at NPC creation/moving a bunch in the same “tick” though with me defining every “tick” as the interval at which packets send). I am confident I could probably push it to 2-3k npcs if the clientside wasn’t a limiting factor and at this point I am just trying to see how far I can take it lol

posts.author: aC_asual
posts.content: I disabled most humanoid features such as states and the nametags. Script wise i made it so that interpolation uses multiple actors and scripts, moving also uses BulkMoveTo.

posts.author: DadHobbitWizard
posts.content: Great system. This is very similar to how it’s done in optimized multiplayer services. I have had to do this in an engine called Shiva, but had to use packed strings because it only had basic data types! Great thought around only sending ROT Y. If the terrain is flat, that’s 3 more not needed too. One further optimization is skipping the object if it hasn’t materially changed state, and then sending master state every X seconds for new joiners. You can also optimize for distance from nearest player if not visible, to reduce ticks. The client interpolation can have more varieties too, depending on how accurate it needs to be. Love to see this kind of stuff! Roblox is versatile

posts.author: me7474
posts.content: In my most recent version of this system I only send changed NPCs, update non critical properties less often than every tenth of a second, and have been playing around with a pooling system I haven’t seen anywhere else. I swapped the remotes to send delta changes for positions instead of full positions, because I am firing every 0.1 seconds there is only so many deltas that will actually be seen per packet. Most of these numbers are duplicates which means at the expense of simplicity you can cut down greatly on network. Basically instead of assigning each NPC a buffer I assign NPC IDs to delta values. Say 150 NPCs had a delta X position of +3, I make a long buffer with all 150 IDs. I know each ID is 11 bits so I don’t need to provide any separators. the packet for this would look like so: {bufferForXDeltas, {bufferForDeltaOne, bufferForDeltaTwo, bufferForDeltaThree, ...}, ...} say my X deltas for this packet are +2, +4, -1. I would include three buffers with IDs that match the deltas, so a psuedocode packet that represents the same structure would look like this: {{2, 4, -1}, {{ID_4, ID_7}, {ID_3}, {ID_1, ID_12, ID_6, ID_5}}, ...} Now I know NPC 4 and 7 have a delta X of +2, NPC 3 a delta of +4, etc. and because I am using deltas and sending frequently there aren’t many X deltas so we cut a ton of deadweight loss attributed to repeating them. I also have thought about replacing the ID buffer containers with a single bit for each NPC given I know the amount of existing NPCs. If I have 8 NPCs alive and ID 2 and 6 have a delta of +1 I can set the +1 key to 01000100 , with the ones corresponding to the IDs that change. I also considered replacing the value keys with binary addition. If we have NPCs that move -1, 0, 1, 2 in a packet we could use half the data by sending a reference saying “hey we are starting at -1” and then only having fields for +1 and +2 as these deltas can form any of the values above. At the end of the day these are just pushing it for the sake of pushing it though, my client performance is already the limiting factor so 0 reason to improve networking I am unsure if these concepts has been done before but I personally couldn’t find anything like them trying to look it up. Considering you have been in the gamedev scene a lot longer, do you know if this is an original set of concepts or commonly used? edit: also worth noting obviously using deltas has the added benefit of needing to allocate less bits per number. The way I make new NPCs/load NPCs for joining players also still uses my old packet system with full info but considering it’s a one time send per NPC it is minimally impactful.

posts.author: DadHobbitWizard
posts.content: That’s clever. Haven’t heard of that specific optimization! Very cool. Pools is good too, we used to do something similar in Asheron’s call in dynamically sized vertical server bands depending on player clustering.

posts.author: aC_asual
posts.content: @me7474 Do you have any tips for using Pathfinding (custom or not) with this large amount of AI?

posts.author: me7474
posts.content: it depends on what usecase you have but if you want them all pathfinding individually with non reused paths that is pretty much out of the question unfortunately. I haven’t looked into pathfinding much but off the top of my head the first two things I would look at would be entity grouping (e.g. if you have 50 enemies all clumped together you want to move from A to B you can basically treat them as a single entity, pathfind for only one and move them as a hoard) and caching of paths (if you expect NPCs to be traveling the same path multiple times save the path so you don’t have to recalculate). It also heavily depends on what you are trying to pathfind across. I’ve done A* algorithm implementations for when I had a terrain system that could be categorized as a grid, for more complex maps though you will likely have to come up with your own solutions

posts.author: aC_asual
posts.content: Okay so, i couldnt really find any good alternatives to Pathfinding (since i want the AI to work in a 3D environment with multiple height levels and other factors), so i resorted to using SimplePath , which turned out pretty well! Managed to get 250 Pathfinding AI without any issues. There’s still alot of room for improvement, but so far everything’s working as intended. As a side note, while messing around with AgentRadius and a Waypoint-Skip mechanic, they created this “crowding” effect where they all clump up and it really sells into the hivemind feeling of these zombies.

posts.author: me7474

posts.author: vantoanvh_dev
posts.content: I have tried to make a system, but will be in the future as a resource. This is like the most impossible thing you can think of simulating 500 NPCs performantly. or even 1000 for sure. This is a pretty complex scene with running, jumping, rotating alot. It supported alot like: Fully support all animation states ( climbing, walking, jumping, ragdoll, teleport, death, … ) and pretty customizable if attack, others animation needed. Yes, it has headers. NPCs moves freely, no boundaries or limited range. 0.2 studs precision for X, Z, 0.5 studs precision for Y, 6 degrees precision for Yaw. 20hz sending ( about 0.05 seconds per sent ) Benchmark: 500 NPCs ran at 11KB/s - 12KB/s, ran pretty performant 1000 NPCs ran at 20KB/s - 21KB/s, a bit lag because of using BulkMoveTo , I am thinking of using Motor6D instead. I tried to made this as an open-source replication framework inspiring Lightweight NPCs for everyone to use, but it may takes lots time to make it easily usable tho

posts.author: awry_y
posts.content: BlueDeveloper24: That comment doesnt help at all. saying “3d flying is unoptimized” doesnt help much. Adding 2 more nodes for +vector3.YAxist and -Vector3.Yaxis is not any different than adding 2 nodes in the X or Z scale. Also you are not helping. If you cant help, please refrain from adding more comments here. I am not even trying to help you. I’ll explain now because you seem a bit new. Why adding two nodes on y axis cause lag? Let’s say your algorithm searches an area 10x10 studs, that’s 100 studs scanned, seem pretty good. Scanning an additional axis would mean 1000 studs and doing this on a larger scale could be devastating. Especially if there are multiple obstacles. This would mean too much lag on computing. Rather, take my approach of using raycasts to see if the next node is above our initial height and then set our height to that specified height which is much more optimised and that’s what I did for my algorithm. posts.author: awry_y
posts.content: Maybe. I am currently on vacation but just giving a basic idea is that you need to cast a ray 7 studs or move above from your presummed next point downwords to about 20 studs and also cast rays from the point where the topray impacted a part to see if there is sufficient space. Also use workspace:GetPartsInRadius to see if that point is actually traversible or not. Or just use raycasts. Basically: parentNode -- our parent node local nodePosition = -- our nodes position local heightRay = Raycast(nodePosition+Vector3.yAxis*10-Vector3.yAxis*20) if heightRay then local upCheckRay = Raycast(heightRay.Position,Vector3.yAxis*7) local collissionRay = Raycast(parentNode.Position+Vector3.yAxis*7,direction) -- where direction is basically where the new node would be. if not upCheckRay and not collissionRay then --make your point end end

items.title: Suphi's DataStore Module

posts.author: 5uphi
posts.content: Suphi's DataStore Module Download | Video Tutorial | Discord Server Features Session locking "Prevents another object from opening the same datastore key while its opened by another object" Cross Server Communication "Easily use MemoryStoreQueue to send data to the session owner" Auto Save "Automatically saves cached data to the datastore based on the saveinterval property" Bind To Close "Automatically saves, closes and destroys all sessions when server starts to close" Reconcile "Fills in missing values from the template into the value property" Compression "Compress data to reduce character count" Multiple Script Support "Safe to interact with the same datastore object from multiple scripts" Task Batching "Tasks will be batched together when possible" Direct Value Access "Access the datastore value directly, module will never tamper with your data and will never leave any data in your datastore or memorystore" Easy To Use "Simple and elegant" Lightweight "No RunService events and no while true do loops 100% event based" Support My Work If you liked my work and want to donate to me you can do so here SourceCode You can get the sourcecode to this module here Permission to use, copy, modify, and/or distribute this software for any purpose with or without fee is hereby granted. Common problems Datastore locked when teleporting or migrating to latest update "simple rule to follow to make sure you dont have this problem" only use DataStoreModule.new() inside PlayerAdded and no where else "how to test if your breaking this rule" local function StateChanged(state, dataStore) if state == nil then print("Destroyed", dataStore.Id) end if state == false then print("Closed ", dataStore.Id) end if state == true then print("Open ", dataStore.Id) end end) game.Players.PlayerAdded:Connect(function(player) local dataStore = DataStoreModule.new("NAME", "KEY") -- edit this dataStore.StateChanged:Connect(StateChanged) if dataStore.StateChanged:Wait() == true then task.wait(math.random(10, 20)) print(player.Name, "datastore is going to be destroyed") dataStore:Destroy() -- if the players datastore opens again -- then you are suffering from this problem -- once you destroy a players datastore -- it should never open again end end) Documentation CONSTRUCTOR new(name: string, scope: string, key: string) "Returns previously created datastore session else a new session" new(name: string, key: string) "Returns previously created datastore session else a new session" hidden(name: string, scope: string, key: string) "Returns a new session that cannot be returned by new or find" hidden(name: string, key: string) "Returns a new session that cannot be returned by new or find" find(name: string, scope: string, key: string) "Returns previously created datastore session else nil" find(name: string, key: string) "Returns previously created datastore session else nil" Response Success Saved Locked State Error "List of responses that acts like a enum" PROPERTIES Value any nil "Value

items.title: Global Matchmaking System with MemoryStoreService

posts.author: livingblu
posts.content: So I used HowToRoblox’s global matchmaking system and it worked great when I tested it in my game . When I published my update, I tested it with thousands of people and it seemed to not teleport me at all. I looked at the developer console and it repeated: MemoryStoreService: RequestThrottled: Recent MemoryStores requests hit one or more limits. API: SortedMap.GetRange, Data Structure: Queue. My game has around 4-8k players, so that might be the problem. The thing is, I’ve seen other games like Bedwars achieve a matchmaking system. So how would I approach this?

items.title: Conceptual Implementation: Building a Custom Matchmaking Service with MemoryStore

posts.author: Lord_Monever
posts.content: Hi everyone, here is a modular approach to designing efficient, scalable matchmaking using MemoryStoreService . I wanted to build a matchmaking module but wasn’t sure where to start. This is my theoretical implementation, which I plan to develop into a full module. Overview This guide outlines the design principles, data flow, and theories for creating a custom matchmaking system using Roblox’s MemoryStore . It is structured to support features such as: Region-based queues Party/group matchmaking Custom implementation of matchmaking functions Cross-server coordination This system is built entirely on MemoryStore , as traditional DataStore usage is currently limited based on the number of players in an individual server. Most matchmaking systems rely on a central coordinator server to manage matchmaking logic across all servers, but this setup can quickly hit DataStore call limits when scaling — for example, with 1,000 players, a small coordinator server wouldn’t be able to keep up. In contrast, MemoryStore supports high-frequency, cross-server access and can handle this load effectively. Memorystore are still affected by rate limits which are talked in terms of “units” (1000 + 100 * [number of concurrent users] request units per minute). Note: Roblox is planning to lift DataStore server-based limits in 2026 , moving to a model based on concurrent users across the entire experience . Becoming the Matchmaker Coordinator Server When a player joins a matchmaker on a server, the matchmaker on that server will begin attempting to claim the role of matchmaker coordinator — a server responsible for managing matchmaking logic - for that mode and that region, as long as there are players in the queue of that server’s matchmaker. Matchmaker Coordinator Election Process Every REFRESH_TIME seconds, a server’s matchmaker with players in queue will attempt to become the Matchmaker Coordinator by calling UpdateAsync on a MemoryStoreHashMap key specific to the matchmaker mode and region: MemoryStoreHashMap:UpdateAsync( key, transformFunction, expiration, ) key : This is the string identifier used to locate a specific entry in the hash map. In the context of matchmaking, this key might represent a specific queue or region (e.g., "ranked-solo-silver-na" ). transformFunction : A function that receives the current value associated with the key and returns a new value to be stored. This function allows atomic updates—ensuring that the value is only changed if it hasn’t been modified by another server in the meantime. expiration : A number representing how many seconds the new value should persist in the store before it automatically expires. This acts as a safeguard—if a server crashes or fails to refresh its claim, the key will eventually become available again. In matchmaking scenarios, this prevents a server from permanently locking the matchmaker role. This should be controlled by a variable called EXPIRATION_TIME . The transformFunction works as follows: If the k
