posts.author: KiwiPG
posts.content: I mean, its possible to technically not use algebra in programming your game. But programming at its core is logic and problem solving, which is the same for math. Even if you don’t use math 1+1, math.abs(5*(-2)) , you game will still be filled with math behind the scenes, and not being able to understand how it works wouldnt get you far with your experience as many many systems require basic math (Player movement, lighting, modeling, etc.)

posts.author: MaxthedaydreamerXMx
posts.content: Did you even… take a single computer science course? Mostly not, but most of the math is relatively simple, and there’s guides by extremely smart people out there, so you should be fine

posts.author: NyrionDev
posts.content: Now with LLMs yes, before LLMs yes for most things. The reason LLMs work so well for math related tasks is that most (if not all) the issues you will ever encounter when writing code have already been encountered many years before, and solved. Unless of course you are doing something absurd. Even if you think that the problem you face has no answer out there it most likely can be reformed to an already solved problem (for example many math problems can often be reformed in such a way so graph theory can be applied to them). If you are stuck you can also do the old trick of splitting a problem to smaller parts and solving them individually (as a means of simplification, this can also help the LLM because it tends to do well with smaller tasks). For example if lets say I want to write an image parser, for example JPEG (so it reads image bytecode from HttpService and converts it to readable pixels in a buffer to show on a UI) I can simply tell the LLM to split the task into many smaller problems, and ask it to solve each problem individually and verify its solution. Then I can feed it back all the small parts it solved and ask it to combine them in a single working module. All this can be done while having absolutely zero mathematical knowledge. Basically dark times we live in.

posts.author: 2112Jay
posts.content: Yes, it is possible to script without math.. Conditions, events, loops , basic comparisons, object manipulation. local Players = game:GetService("Players") Players.PlayerAdded:Connect(function(player) player.CharacterAdded:Connect(function(character) local humanoid = character:WaitForChild("Humanoid") humanoid.Died:Connect(function() print(player.Name .. " died") end) end) end) I’ve seen boot-sector games made like this with no real math at all. They defined colors.. blue + red = green .. kind of thing.

posts.author: Ethacon_Dev

posts.author: verva1_n
posts.content: This is definitely possible! There will be a few times where you may need a little equation but it’s as simple as either checking the Dev fourm or asking ai to figure out a calculation for you (which usually works out pretty nice!)

posts.author: pullman45
posts.content: Math is just logic with numbers. If you can think logically then you can do math.

posts.author: L0chlainn
posts.content: Scripting is essentially just logic, data and math. So yes, you can script without math and the most you will need in a day to day basis can be figured out by asking around or using AI. You should know this though, algebra is essentially a key part of programming, whether you’re aware of it or not. If you know one, you know the other, even if you don’t realise it. There is a catch to this answer however, which shouldn’t be sugar coated… Math is fundementally a core skill in programming, so when you don’t understand that core skill you’ll, be inclined to make poor design choices, as you won’t understand how to derive meaningful measurements from the data you have. That wont appear for most of your day to day, but it’s something that’ll present itself sooner or later. You can of course use AI to handle much of it these days, but you can’t exactly search for something when you don’t even know you should be searching for it. AI is only as good as the questions you ask it. In game developing especially, there’s only so much you can do with logic and piping data. Sooner or later, you’ll need to derive your own measurements from that data or create new data which may require algebra, statistics, calculus, geometry and/or matrices (This is what a CFrame essentially is). So honestly, don’t lie to yourself, but don’t over think it either. Math is going to be something you will sooner or later have to pick up, but it’s not as daunting of a task as you may think. Khan Academy and the internet in general is your friend, you can find so many people in communities like Robloxian Mathematical Society who couldn’t be more eager to help you learn even the basics. You should also look online for text books on Amazon or free PDFs, you’d be amazed at how superior they are at teaching compared to what schools provide.

posts.author: NyrionDev
posts.content: L0chlainn: but you can’t exactly search for something when you don’t even know you should be searching for it Actually as long you have a core idea (for example what you want to optimize) and you provide AI with your code, and ask it specifically to figure out ideas to achieve what you asked, it can figure out mathematical tricks, suggest them and then apply them to your code. You can even ask it to find the key bottlenecks in your code, or identify bugs, or figure out why specific inputs create wrong outputs. AI isn’t perfect, but it has gotten really good recently, especially Claude and Gemini. On top of that, ChatGPT may not be that good for specific things, but compared to other AIs it has a “good eye”, in the sense that even if it can’t fix your code as precisely as Claude, it can notice things you didn’t even ask it to notice, and mention them to you. Another thing I have noticed is that sometimes what really helps out LLMs is to push them down a specific intelligence / specialization path before asking for something. For example if I want to crack a hash, I wont ask it to crack a hash first, I will first ask it for linux and cybersecurity related things. This also helps with defending many of the “safety” mechanisms known AIs have to deny you access to information. For example if you start talking about virology to an AI before asking it to engineer a virus, it is more likely to do it than asking for that directly. It reminds me a bit of how you make humans confess to things by having a chit chat with them first and breaking the ice.

posts.content: https://devforum.roblox.com/t/task-library-now-available/1387845 image 997×665 42.9 KB wait throttles at 30hz, meaning it will not leverage greater precision and can yield for longer than requested. task.wait leverages frame/simulation rate, which enables it to improve the accuracy at which your thread sleeps for the targeted amount of time.

items.title: How can I properly make anti-cheat for a +1 Speed Escape Obby?

posts.author: exvirus2
posts.content: Hello, I’m currently making a +1 Speed Escape Obby, but the only thing I’m not sure how to handle correctly is the anti-cheat system. I want to prevent players from using things like: Flying Speed hacks Teleporting / noclip Other exploits that let them skip levels even when they don’t have enough speed The main goal is to make sure players: Can’t pass levels if they don’t actually have enough speed Can’t claim rewards by exploiting (Stage Rewards) Does anyone have any good ideas or best practices for handling this properly?

posts.author: EzraPalo
posts.content: handle as much as possible on server except animations, or at least the validation

posts.author: Code_PRANKSTER
posts.content: I have a possible solution for this anti-cheat system of yours: You could design the anti-cheat to constantly verify that a player’s actual movement matches their in-game stats, ensuring their character hasn’t been modified. For example, if someone only has +3 speed but is moving far faster due to a cheat modification, the system would detect that mismatch and flag it. I’d also recommend building in multiple layers of checks and safeguards so the anti-cheat is harder to bypass. For flying, you can handle it by validating whether the player is grounded or within expected vertical movement limits. Track how long a player stays in the air and compare it to what’s normally possible. For teleporting and noclip, you can handle it by keeping an eye on how players move between updates. If someone suddenly jumps way farther than they should based on their speed, that’s a pretty clear sign something’s off, so you can flag them or move them back. For noclip, you can check if a player is in a wall, which will flag them. I’d highly recommend making the anti-cheat kick the player out instead of perma-banning, due to false positives if a player was stuck without the use of cheats (similar to Forsaken).

posts.author: Code_PRANKSTER
posts.content: By the way, when you’re working on the flying phase of the anti-cheat, make sure the anti-cheat doesn’t flag players falling instead of flying. I’ve made this same mistake before, and had to scrap the whole idea.

posts.author: tytippy
posts.content: Hello! What you could do for the fly hacks is add an invisible wall above the obby, and if the user touches it, they get kicked from the game or temporarily banned. For the speed hacks that could be done by detecting, for example, if the user’s speed is above the speed that they should be at, then they get kicked, so for example, if the user is meant to have 30 speed, if they have anything above that, then they get kicked. Here is a little example of the speed hack anti-cheat that you could develop. Put this in a server script and in serverScriptService local maxspeed = 15 -- you can set this to be dynamic if needed local chances = 3 local personschances = {} game.Players.PlayerAdded:Connect(function(player) personschances[player] = 0 player.CharacterAdded:Connect(function(char) local human = char:WaitForChild("Humanoid") print("working") while char.Parent do print("worked but just waiting") task.wait(5) if human.WalkSpeed maxspeed then if personschances[player] == 3 then player:Kick("Kicked") else personschances[player] = personschances[player] + 1 human.WalkSpeed = maxspeed -- This changes the users speed back to the max speed print("SHOW A WARNING UI TO THE USER TO NOTIFY THEM") print("Users chances are ".. personschances[player].. "/3") end else print("Players speed is normal") end end end) end) game.Players.PlayerRemoving:Connect(function(player) personschances[player] = nil -- removes the players chances :) end) What this does is check whether the user’s speed exceeds the maximum speed. If it is, it sets their speed back to the max speed and gives them a “warning,” then if they get 3 warnings, they get kicked. This is a very basic script to give an idea. If there is anything I can help with, feel free to reply, and I will help you when I can.

posts.author: Wingtips
posts.content: This method does not work; when an exploiter changes their speed locally , your server-sided script will not detect that change. The method posted by @2112Jay will suffer the same fate. Writing this as a LocalScript is borderline pointless, as any exploiter educated enough could just remove that script locally. Adjusting WalkSpeed isn’t the only way people go faster, either. You can use the code below to test. Put it in ServerScriptService Run the game Change your speed locally (Workspace Character Humanoid WalkSpeed = 100) Nothing happens, your speed is still 100. Now, switch to the server view while playing (looks like a little monitor, icon below) and change your character’s speed there. A print statement will be outputted and the character’s speed will be changed. image 165×100 3.55 KB Code local Players = game:GetService("Players") local DEFAULT_WALKSPEED = 16 local MAX_WALKSPEED = 20 local function onPlayerAdded(player: Player) local function onCharacterAdded(character: Model) task.wait() local humanoid = character:WaitForChild("Humanoid") if not humanoid then return end humanoid.Changed:Connect(function(property: string) if property ~= "WalkSpeed" then return end print("Player", player.Name .. "'s WalkSpeed changed to", humanoid.WalkSpeed) local magnitude = humanoid.MoveDirection.Magnitude if magnitude 0 then humanoid.WalkSpeed = math.min(DEFAULT_WALKSPEED, MAX_WALKSPEED) else humanoid.WalkSpeed = DEFAULT_WALKSPEED end end) end if player.Character then onCharacterAdded(player.Character) end player.CharacterAdded:Connect(onCharacterAdded) end Players.PlayerAdded:Connect(onPlayerAdded)

items.title: [1000 ROBUX BOUNTY] Super buggy pathfinding object - moves in random places, disappears randomly

posts.author: eljefeistheboss
posts.content: NOTE: I say that the issue only occurs when you die and respawn, but for some reason it can also happen when you die and keep moving? It’s very odd. Just keep that in mind. I am currently trying to make a system where a part chases you. My method of doing this is constantly moving it where it is facing, and updating its orientation, based on pathfinding waypoints. This way, it will always reach the player, and not lag behind it. For the most part, it works fine, until I die. If I stand still after I respawn, it calculates the path again and everything is fine. However, if I move? after death, it literally just goes in one direction forever, only printing one thing: "Found target - updating position. Target: CinematicCapybara CFrame Target: 117.419762, 4.00006104, -71.104599, 1, 0, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0, 1 " (not the EXACT same thing.) No errors. No warnings. Just this. Over and over again. It’s driving me crazy. I’ve tried every solution, but no matter what I do, this bug just keeps popping up, is really hard to replicate, and seemingly can not be prevented at all. This is my last resort. Here is the entire script [Module] local module = {} -- VARS local rs = game:GetService("RunService") local ps = game:GetService("PathfindingService") local playingTeam = game.Teams:WaitForChild("Playing") local brickSignal = game.Workspace:WaitForChild("brickSignal") local visualFolder = game.Workspace:WaitForChild("waypointVisualFolder") -- FUNCS function module.Initalize(brick:Part, speed:number, damage:number, deathmsg:string) -- brick functions warn("Brick initalized") local currentWaypoint = nil local closestDistance = math.huge local currentTargetPlayer:Player = nil local currentTargetCFrame:CFrame = CFrame.new(0,0,0) local lastCurrentTargetCFrame:CFrame = CFrame.new(0,0,99) local function getClosestPlayer() -- Find the closest player (returns nothing) closestDistance = math.huge for i,player:Player in pairs(game.Players:GetPlayers()) do if player then local char = player.Character if not char then warn("No player character found.") continue end local hrp:Part = char:FindFirstChild("HumanoidRootPart") local hum:Humanoid = char:FindFirstChild("Humanoid") local playerTeam = player.Team if playerTeam == playingTeam then if hrp and hum then if hum.Health 0 then local dist = (hrp.Position - brick.Position).Magnitude if dist = closestDistance then closestDistance = dist currentTargetPlayer = player else warn("The selected character is not farther away than the current one.") end else warn("The selected character is dead.") end else warn("The selected character is missing either a HumanoidRootPart or a Humanoid") end else warn("Player is not on Playing team.") end else warn("Could not find player!") return nil end end end local function checkTargetAndMove() if currentTargetPlayer then local char = currentTargetPlayer.Character if char then local hrp = char:WaitForChild("HumanoidRootPart") local hum = char:WaitForChild("Humanoid") if not hrp then warn("Player does not

posts.author: happya_x
posts.content: The main issue is your method of updating reachedWaypoint. checkTargetAndMove pushes the part forward 3 studs every 0.05 seconds, then pathFindToPlayer waits until it reaches the waypoint. If the part moves too fast and overshoots the waypoint, then reachedWaypoint never becomes true and then pathFindToPlayer freezes the thread so getClosestPlayer is never called. Solution: In your pathfinding, generate a list of Vector3 points and put them in a table. Then keep an index tracking which waypoint you are currently on. Unify the loops into one loop connected to Runservice.Heartbeat that will both calculate the direction to the current waypoint and then push the part forward dynamically based on deltaTime. You get faster and better detection if you switch to if (Part.Position - Target.Position).Magnitude distance instead of checking if its inside the bounds of the part Something like this RunService.Heartbeat:Connect(function(deltaTime: number) currentTargetPlayer = GetClosestPlayer() if not currentTargetPlayer or not currentTargetPlayer.Character then return end local character: Model = currentTargetPlayer.Character local humanoidRootPart: BasePart = character:FindFirstChild("HumanoidRootPart") :: BasePart local humanoid: Humanoid = character:FindFirstChild("Humanoid") :: Humanoid if not humanoidRootPart or not humanoid or humanoid.Health = 0 then return end -- Asynchronously recalculate the path at a fixed rate to prevent stalling the thread if os.clock() - lastPathCalculationTime PATH_RECALCULATION_RATE then lastPathCalculationTime = os.clock() task.spawn(CalculatePathToTarget, humanoidRootPart.Position) end -- Do movement and steering logic executed in a single step if currentWaypointIndex = #currentWaypoints then local targetWaypoint: PathWaypoint = currentWaypoints[currentWaypointIndex] local direction: Vector3 = (targetWaypoint.Position - brick.Position) local distanceToWaypoint: number = direction.Magnitude if distanceToWaypoint 0.001 then local moveVector: Vector3 = direction.Unit * speed * deltaTime if distanceToWaypoint = WAYPOINT_REACHED_THRESHOLD then currentWaypointIndex += 1 else brick.CFrame = CFrame.lookAt(brick.Position + moveVector, targetWaypoint.Position) end end else -- Move directly toward the player if no waypoints exist (direct line of sight) local direction: Vector3 = (humanoidRootPart.Position - brick.Position) if direction.Magnitude 0.001 then local moveVector: Vector3 = direction.Unit * speed * deltaTime brick.CFrame = CFrame.lookAt(brick.Position + moveVector, humanoidRootPart.Position) end end -- Hit detection using Magnitude over spatial query local distanceFromPlayer: number = (humanoidRootPart.Position - brick.Position).Magnitude if distanceFromPlayer = WAYPOINT_REACHED_THRESHOLD then if os.clock() - lastDamageTime = DAMAGE_COOLDOWN then lastDamageTime = os.clock() humanoid:TakeDamage(damage) print(deathMessage) end end end) Better example for getClosestPlayer local function getClosestPlayer(): Player? local closestD

items.title: How do developers organize their projects so it is scalable?

posts.author: GotMilkBoyz
posts.content: Background Info: I have been learning to program on Roblox Studio for a while now, and after years of coming back and learning I feel like I am at around the level between intermediate and advanced, and going forward I want to get in the habit of developing good practices when it comes to organizing how I develop my mini-projects. Before I considered learning organization, I usually just put server scripts or clients scripts where they are suppose to be without having structure, and even though it works, I knew there was no way it was scalable or flexible. What I am trying to learn: At the moment, I am trying to see how people organize their projects, and while I have watched some YouTube Videos on this topic and feel like I have gained some understanding on how basic structures and practices should be done (like using folders, ModuleScripts, and where scripts should be), I still have some questions that were either never answered in the videos or were just too hard to understand. I know key aspects of a good architecture include things like: Services, Controllers, Managers, Handlers, but what do they even mean/do and how do they actually play a part in your project? What roles do module loaders play in projects, and why are they preferred over requiring modules manually everywhere? I probably have more qusetions that I can’t remember right now, but those are the two important ones I think Note: I forgot to say this, but I am trying to learn single-script architecture. heard there was a big difference between single and multi so its probably important to know what im tryna learn

posts.author: xnlogical
posts.content: CutiePatootie: I know key aspects of a good architecture include things like: Services, Controllers, Managers, Handlers, but what do they even mean/do and how do they actually play a part in your project? Service: typically refers to a unit of behavior that exclusively runs on the server. Controller: typically refers to a unit of behavior that exclusively runs on the client. Manager nor handler really mean anything on their own. CutiePatootie: What roles do module loaders play in projects, and why are they preferred over requiring modules manually everywhere? That’s kind of a bad description of modern module loaders. They don’t actually wrap require anymore, they only manage lifecycles of services/controllers. This ranges from initialization, starting, to deinitialization. CutiePatootie: Note: I forgot to say this, but I am trying to learn single-script architecture. heard there was a big difference between single and multi so its probably important to know what im tryna learn What you described is SSA. Multi-script architecture is only bad because it leads to the isolation of behavior and by extension repetition redundancy.

posts.author: Vyntrick
posts.content: Since this is already solved, I’ll just chime in with something I really love about SSA compared to MSA. If I had to share a bit more details about SSA (probably not standard practice but does wonders for me) is when a single script requires all the modules at runtime with the Loader module, all the required modules share the same copy of the require. Typically, if you were to require a ModuleScript in MSA, the first require is cached. So, if there was one script elsewhere that called require, that script will only return the first requires results, even if the other script called a method stored within the ModuleScript that may overwrite the values that the other script also tried to access. This would essentially prevent calls to the ModuleScript from overwriting it’s own values with it’s own methods so other scripts can get those updated values (not to be confused with a script forcefully overwriting a modules values). The reason why this is so helpful is because you can expose tables and specific variables stored within the ModuleScript to other ModuleScripts in the game. Since they were all required by one script either on the Client or Server, they all can access and change each others values. I get this probably isn’t safe, however it’s super helpful so if I use a method from inside one of the Services / Controllers, other Modules can immediately get that value without there needing to be Value objects, middle-mans, or some other stupidly hacky setup just to give a value to another module. Sometimes I don’t always want a static value. I’ve been heavily using this for an upcoming project of mine and it has worked so effectively, I have zero complaints. (Also so much less hassle compared to that of MSA, but that’s my preference talking)

posts.author: GotMilkBoyz
posts.content: xnlogical: That’s kind of a bad description of modern module loaders. They don’t actually wrap require anymore, they only manage lifecycles of services/controllers. This ranges from initialization, starting, to deinitialization. I saw this at like 11pm so I didn’t think this as thoroughly, but isn’t this description basically a bootstrapper?

posts.author: xnlogical
posts.content: Yes, but the terms themselves are largely interchangable, I just prefer “module loader” because it’s slightly less vague.

items.title: How would I make an arrow projectile curve tween?

posts.author: Lightning_Fan12345
posts.content: So yeah I have the Arrow Position and the Mouse Position and I know how to tween from point A → point B but how would I make it curve up and come down like this image? I cannot comprehend the math that would go into making this D: ArrowCurveTweenImage 1492×942 6.56 KB i know, it’s a masterpiece B]

posts.author: Denzil160
posts.content: Hi, user LightningFan, here is a solution or an idea for this to help: To create a curved projectile path in Roblox Studio using a “tween” style, the most effective method is using a Quadratic Bezier Curve . Standard TweenService only moves objects in a straight line. By using a Bezier formula, you can interpolate between a start point, a “peak” (control point), and an endpoint. The Math To find the position at any time (t) (where (0) is the start and (1) is the end), use this formula: (P(t)=(1-t)^{2}P_{0}+2(1-t)tP_{1}+t^{2}P_{2}) (P_{0}) : Start Position (The Archer) (P_{1}) : Control Point (The “Height” of the arc) (P_{2}) : End Position (The Mouse/Target) Implementation in ScriptSince TweenService can’t move parts along a curve directly, you can tween a NumberValue from 0 to 1 and use the .Changed event to update the arrow’s position.lualocal TweenService = game:GetService(“TweenService”) local function lerp(p0, p1, t) return p0 + (p1 - p0) * t end local function quadBezier(t, p0, p1, p2) local l1 = lerp(p0, p1, t) local l2 = lerp(p1, p2, t) return lerp(l1, l2, t) end – Setup local startPos = arrow.Position local endPos = mouse.Hit.p local controlPoint = startPos:Lerp(endPos, 0.5) + Vector3.new(0, 20, 0) – Adjust 20 for height local tValue = Instance.new(“NumberValue”) local tween = TweenService:Create(tValue, TweenInfo.new(2), {Value = 1}) tValue.Changed:Connect(function(t) local newPos = quadBezier(t, start Pos, controlPoint, endPos) – Optional: Make the arrow point in the direction it’s moving local nextPos = quadBezier(t + 0.01, startPos, controlPoint, endPos) arrow.CFrame = CFrame.new(newPos, nextPos) end) tween:Play() tween.Completed:Connect(function() Pro Tips Dynamic Height: Set the “controlPoint” height based on the distance between the archer and the target so the arc looks natural. Orientation: As shown in the code, calculate a tiny bit ahead ((t + 0.01)) to find the “look at” direction so the arrow doesn’t stay horizontal during the flight. Physics Alternative: If you want the arrow to interact with walls/players mid-flight, consider using the FastCast module or applying a LinearVelocity with workspace gravity.

posts.author: Amritss
posts.content: You can just do: local MaxRange = -- Choose your max range here local direction = (originPosition - BeamEnd.Position).Unit * MaxRange The unit property on vectors copies the exact same vector but with a magnitude of 1. Its useful for raycasting because you can specify the range. That’s what people would do for guns where you don’t want someone to be able to land a shot from like 1k+ studs away or something. Or if you’re feeling lazy you can just… pcall. It would stop your scirpt from breaking but the code inside wouldn’t run so your humanoid wouldn’t detect. So yeah just do the first thing I said.

posts.author: ivan866_z
posts.content: you cannot detect Humanoid, for it is not a geometric object what you can detect with raycast is parts, meshparts, decals, and clothes you are trying to find humanoid by traversing the parent of the raycast instance, then using FindFirstChild this is where it fails correct approach: get list of hit parts, get their Parent property use Players service’s method GetPlayerFromCharacter on EACH of them; store in table make the table items unique (either implement yourself or use a general purpose library for that) now you have a list of hit Player objects (they are NOT Character models, and NOT Humanoid objects) once you get your Player object, its player.Character and Character.Humanoid properties will be the ones you can actually use (notice getting those very early in the loading stage usually doesn’t work and requires .CharacterAppearanceLoaded.Wait() method) [only the green objects can be detected with raycast] (the diagram is not full, obviously, but it shows the principal structure of the Player object) image 738×399 11.2 KB notice Character.Parent will not return you the Player object, because the Character will be parented to the workspace at runtime

posts.author: emilisyaye
posts.content: Well it checks if the PARENT has a name called “Humanoid” and if it does then it will continue what it’s doing. And since its doing whats its suppose to do then its fine

posts.author: ivan866_z
posts.content: typical ‘works on my machine’ philosophy i wrote you down the robust, correct approach for the problem just works. every. time. also notice, the approach with FindFirstChild forces your script to traverse the whole hierarchy of multiple found objects, potentially hundreds, on each raycast while the Players service, most likely, uses a hashmap underneath, simply returning you the player which possesses the required character model (which is described by a single four-byte guid)

posts.author: emilisyaye
posts.content: well if ill be honest it does work for them too, only issue is with math (fixed by now) and how its detecting since Spherecast only returns one instance

posts.author: ivan866_z
posts.content: if you need detecting multiple objects along the way of your ‘beam’, raycast is not the right thing to do you must use a thin cylinder for that and call workspace: GetPartsInPart (cylinder, params) notice it is a much more expensive operation

posts.author: emilisyaye

For the humanoid part, you are correct but you can just use FindFirstChildOfClass() since its more robust than checking the name.

posts.author: ivan866_z

items.title: Classes added to Luau?

posts.author: Archbarren
posts.content: Wasn’t really sure where to put this but I was scrolling Twitter and I found a post talking about how classes were added to Luau? https://x.com/i/status/2054654445296742437 Is this real?? Can it be used in Roblox???

posts.author: Archbarren
posts.content: See also: The actual website talking about it Luau RFCs RFCs RFCs for Luau evolution

posts.author: xnlogical
posts.content: This is an RFC (request for comments). Which is primarily a proposal, not just a feature plan, meaning it may or may not ever get ratified. I’d say this has a very low chance at that, I just doesn’t see the Luau design team ever finding this compatible with Luau’s purpose. People already heavily objected to const , so classes are a harder if not impossible sell to that kind of minimalist-extremism. I checked the RFC’s PR and it seems to have been merged, which I find very surprising. As when it comes to availability, the RFC does not contain any information, and it also does not seem to work in studio (stable channel). I’m not sure where the person in the X post got access, there doesn’t seem to be a beta feature either.

posts.author: TenebrisNoctua
posts.content: The RFC has been made after implementation to solidify the existence of classes and improve the feature, actually. The current version, which is inaccessible to users unless they find the method to enable it, isn’t up-to-date with the RFC. It soon will be, however.

posts.author: xnlogical
posts.content: I’m still surprised classes were accepted into Luau, let alone implemented.

posts.author: TrustRequiem
posts.content: Originally, the plan was to have shared “self” types on methods. That system was not it… So, we get real classes instead. OOP is quite useful in game development.

posts.author: xnlogical
posts.content: I’ve heard about SST some time ago, and also thought it was too complex for the problem it was trying to solve. Classes are quite interesting though, I’d like to see how they approach inheritance polymorphism later on. However I don’t like how unstable these features tend to be upon release, the new type solver is still extremely unstable to this day; hopefully this will be an exception.

posts.author: ChiDj123
posts.content: RFC is not for request for comments what are u blud on about it stands for Request for change and the RFC is garbage, classes are for losers who are way too addicted to metatable oop

posts.author: TenebrisNoctua
posts.content: RFCs are officially called “Request For Comments” and the Luau team has officially confirmed this from time to time in certain PRs and other comments in other social media platforms. The RFC has also been created by one of the maintainers of the Luau language, and it has been created to improve the already WIP implementation. And the classes feature are not prototype metatable OOP either, it’s a new primitive.

posts.author: xnlogical
posts.content: Luau doesn’t define what “RFC” stands for (at least not in documentation), and in other ecosystems it indeed does stand for “request for comments”: The “RFC” (request for comments) process is intended to provide a consistent and controlled path for changes to Rust (such as new features) so that all stakeholders can be confident about the direction of the project. Source: Rust RFC book You should probably read things before trashing on them. The RFC does not propose syntax sugar over metatables, it proposes an entirely new datatype. I don’t understand why people violently hate OOP just because it tends to be misused, even though bad code very often has nothing to do with which paradigm it is written in. If you don’t like it, don’t use it- it’s as simple as that.

posts.author: ChiDj123
posts.content: elaborate how RFC stands for “Request For Comments” instead of “Request For Change” when that’s what people do in RFCs (request for changes in luau language) ?

posts.author: xnlogical
posts.content: This RFC’s main concern isn’t performance either, it primarily addresses the fact that methods don’t compose well into the type system as per the indirection metatables as a feature introduce; which by the way, plagues cases in which inferring self is otherwise trivial. So at its core this RFC has nothing to do with OOP, it’s primary purpose is to bound self types without introducing absurd amounts of type-system-level complexity. It also potentially opens up the way for the sunsetting of metatables, which is arguably a net positive for the language. Please take your shallow takes somewhere else.

posts.author: TenebrisNoctua
posts.content: Metatables will not be sunset, as classes have metamethods as well. And userdata still utilizes metatables.

posts.author: xnlogical
posts.content: I did not say “classes have nothing to do with OOP”, I said "this RFC has nothing to do with OOP. OOP already exists in Luau for the most part, and this is mostly meant to clean up the technical debt inherited from Lua’s pseudo-OOP. Also please adjust your grammar if you’re going to keep trying to correct me. It’s very hard to read your posts. And maybe tune down the personal attacks as well, all they do is make you less credible, this isn’t texting.

posts.author: xnlogical
posts.content: I didn’t say they will be, I meant to imply that the ratification of this RFC is a big step forward in decreasing the dependence on metatables with actual modern solutions, which by extension may lead to the sunsetting of metatables eventually . Metamethods as justification for the existence of metatables is also weak because in actuality they are decoupled from the indirection inheritance aspect of metatables (which is what’s bad about them), they can work well as pure methods on classes as proven by a lot of languages (in terms of operator overloading). And let’s not pretend that userdata isn’t pseudo-classes anyway, it can easily be deprecated in favor of the new object primitive (depending on its semantics of course). Overall though it heavily depends on how they approach inheritance on classes, my guess is it won’t be anything like metatables due to the fact that classes aren’t prototype based.

posts.author: TenebrisNoctua
posts.content: xnlogical: Metamethods as justification for the existence of metatables is also weak because in actuality they are decoupled from the indirection inheritance aspect of metatables (which is what’s bad about them), they can work well as pure methods on classes as proven by a lot of languages (in terms of operator overloading). And let’s not pretend that userdata isn’t pseudo-classes anyway, it can easily be deprecated in favor of the new object primitive (depending on its semantics of course). Metatables are one of the core aspects of the language, and are not getting removed. Mainly for backwards compatibility and usage that comes from runtime embedders. In fact, maintainers have argued that userdata with metatables be used still instead of class and objects in certain cases. You also seem to not know what metatables and userdata truly are and I advise you to do research.

posts.author: xnlogical
posts.content: Noctua: Metatables are one of the core aspects of the language, and are not getting removed. Okay? As I’ve pointed out twice, this is not what I said. Sunsetting or even deprecation is totally distinct from removal. Noctua: Mainly for backwards compatibility and usage that comes from runtime embedders. Those two things are extremely loose justifications to declare a feature “core”, don’t you think? Besides, that’s exactly what’s being challenged here. Please remember that pairs , ipairs , and next are/were also “core” features. Noctua: In fact, maintainers have argued that userdata with metatables be used still instead of class and objects in certain cases. Maybe because the current concept of classes in Luau is explicitly incomplete? The RFC literally declares private out of scope! As I’ve stated before, all of it heavily depends on how they do inheritance on classes. Noctua: You also seem to not know what metatables and userdata truly are and I advise you to do research. I’ve embedded Lua a few times before, I’m confident I understand what userdata is and I trust that years of scripting has also taught me what metatables metamethods are.

items.title: How would I make a player have anti-gravity?

posts.author: AsianRiceGod666
posts.content: Nothing I try really works. I’ve tried using LinearVelocity and VectorForces but those don’t work. If I give every basepart in the player’s character a VectorForce of part:GetMass() * workspace.Gravity, the player has moon gravity rather than no gravity. I don’t want to set the games gravity , I only want to make a specific player have no gravity.

posts.author: Ionypex23
posts.content: Attach a BodyForce to each part of the character and set Force = part:GetMass() * workspace.Gravity and negate it (or just set it to 0). That cancels gravity for that player only. A BodyVelocity with a constant velocity also works if you want to ignore gravity entirely. No need to touch the global workspace.Gravity .

posts.author: AsianRiceGod666
posts.content: I dont really wanna use a BodyForce because its deprecated. Is there any other way?

posts.author: Ionypex23
posts.content: local char = game.Players.LocalPlayer.Character for _, part in ipairs(char:GetDescendants()) do if part:IsA("BasePart") then -- remove the normal gravity by overriding the BodyVelocity local bv = Instance.new("BodyVelocity") bv.Velocity = Vector3.new(0,0,0) bv.MaxForce = Vector3.new(0,0,0) bv.Parent = part end end Just drop a BodyVelocity with zero velocity and force on every part, that makes the physics engine ignore gravity for that character. No BodyForce needed.

posts.author: Ionypex23
posts.content: There’s no built‑in alternative – keep using BodyForce or just set a BodyVelocity that cancels gravity on each part.

posts.author: AsianRiceGod666
posts.content: I see. Both BodyForce and BodyVelocity are deprecated so I’m really hesitant on using them.

posts.author: Ionypex23
posts.content: No, there is no built‑in alternative. The only way is to use a BodyForce/BodyVelocity (or the equivalent VectorForce/LinearVelocity) on each part. That is the only way to cancel gravity for a single player.

posts.author: benvoiant
posts.content: VectorForce and LinearVelocity are the new versions of BodyForce and BodyVelocity which you should use as alternatives to the deprecated versions. Are you using AI to provide responses to this post?

posts.author: Ionypex23
posts.content: VectorForce and LinearVelocity are the new names for BodyForce and BodyVelocity, they’re still deprecated and you’ll still need to use them (or a BodyVelocity that cancels gravity) to get a single‑player anti‑gravity effect. I’m not AI, just me.

posts.author: benvoiant
posts.content: They’re not just new names, they’re new instances entirely, they were brought in to replace BodyVelocity BodyForce as the new physics solver for the new physics engine. See here

posts.author: Ionypex23
posts.content: LinearVelocity is just the renamed BodyVelocity with the same API, not a brand‑new instance type. It still cancels gravity in the same way you’d use a BodyVelocity.

posts.author: AsianRiceGod666
posts.content: Honestly its not that big of a deal if an AI helps. As long as it answers the question correctly, theres no downside. I see it as a glorified google search.

posts.author: benvoiant
posts.content: But the OP’s question was about alternatives to use instead of BodyForce/Velocity, which are the new VectorForce and LinearVelocity.

posts.author: benvoiant
posts.content: I don’t mind using AI or seeing it be used either to help formulate responses but it appears as if these responses are being directly pasted from an AI source itself… a lot of the answers from this user appear baffling to me and make no sense. The back peddling earlier also has left me skeptical.

posts.author: Ionypex23
posts.content: Use a BodyForce with an upward force equal to gravity times mass, or a BodyVelocity that constantly counters gravity. That gives the player anti‑gravity. Skip the deprecated parts and stick to the new physics instances.

posts.author: Laqotaa
posts.content: It’s called Forum Farming. Report him and ignore. AI isn’t wrong though. Anti-gravity is just cancelling out every force. Instead of following the code AI generated for him cause he’s lazy to learn programming and is just farming DevForum reputation, here is some code stuff for VectorForce. This is just like an example; probably gotta change a few things for your project. local character = script.Parent local VectorForce = Instance.new("VectorForce",character) :: VectorForce local root = character:WaitForChild("HumanoidRootPart") :: BasePart local att = Instance.new("Attachment",root) :: Attachment VectorForce.Attachment0 = att function update() -- So we're rallying up the mass of the entire character, so Instead of creating a VectorForce for each part we just need one on the HumanoidRootPart. local mass = 0 for _,part in pairs(character:GetDescendants()) do if part:IsA("BasePart") then mass += part:GetMass() end end -- Then you just apply it like this. If you apply it on X and Z then you won't be able to move around unless pushed. That is good for jetpacks and stuff. VectorForce.Force = Vector3.new(0,workspace.Gravity * mass,0) end -- Then we update it every Physics Step. game:GetService("RunService").Stepped:Connect(update)

items.title: AI advice for code

posts.author: 1_GeorgeDevs
posts.content: Just need some advice on using ai to code. Im not a great scripter, im ok with basics but struggle with bigger systems. I made a quest system the other day and used ai to tell me all of the structure and instructions without showing me exact code. I copied little to nothing. Should i be using ai this much?

posts.author: darkarai1234
posts.content: I think Ai is a great resource, but I’ve started to notice some problems with my own workflow. I would have Claude give me structure advice, and try to learn from that, but I noticed 2 things. (This is my experience with Ai) AI can be pretty inconsistent with the advice it gives. I was working on a ui system, and it advised me to create a networker for my ui, and then a couple days later I was asking it for some improvements, and it told me the networker wasn’t useful. I think part of that is due to my ineptness to know what it envisioned using the networker for, but not knowing how to use the networker seems like a warning sign. I lost critical thinking skills. By relying on Claude to give me the structure of the code I found myself unable and unwilling to think about a problem for more than a couple minutes. “Why should I devote so much time when Claude has the answer?”. I’ve been taking a break from using AI everyday. I’ll still ask occasional questions, and ask it for code examples, but I’ve been trying to wean off it a bit and relearn how to sit with a problem. My solution: I’ve been trying to read more on forums for my questions, and read more books. I picked up Game Programming Patterns by Robert Nystrom and have been really enjoying learning things I heard Claude mention, but I never really understood. Sitting with problems has been super fun and engaging, and i’m finding that I understand my systems a lot better! Ultimately, you might learn differently from me, but these are just some of my thoughts. Hopefully they help you think about whether or not you should be using Ai as often. Extra thing: I think it’s similar to tutorial hell. Eventually you have to bite the bullet, and just have to dive in.

posts.author: Godzilla_RealLife
posts.content: AI should be rather used as an assistant than building your entire game directly as luaU is easy to learn

posts.author: Denzil160
posts.content: Your post has helped with the question that i tried to figure out on my game by scripting, your post is excellent and scientifically detailed.

posts.author: xnlogical
posts.content: Firekeeper: AI can be pretty inconsistent with the advice it gives. I was working on a ui system, and it advised me to create a networker for my ui, and then a couple days later I was asking it for some improvements, and it told me the networker wasn’t useful. I think part of that is due to my ineptness to know what it envisioned using the networker for, but not knowing how to use the networker seems like a warning sign. AI is literally made to agree with you, it will go anywhere you take the topic/conversation to. You should only take small pieces of advice from it in a fragmented manner, it’s definitely not a good idea to do otherwise because AI will often give you false “peer” reviews in order to agree with you which makes you feel validated, and by extension gives you (potentially) false confidence in more significant decisions. Firekeeper: I lost critical thinking skills. By relying on Claude to give me the structure of the code I found myself unable and unwilling to think about a problem for more than a couple minutes. “Why should I devote so much time when Claude has the answer?”. I’ve been taking a break from using AI everyday. I’ll still ask occasional questions, and ask it for code examples, but I’ve been trying to wean off it a bit and relearn how to sit with a problem. Obviously I don’t know you or your experience with AI, but I doubt this actually happened. It reads to me as if you didn’t lose anything but rather found a new happy path for thinking through problems. I think it’s very important to make the distinction here because this part is a bit fearmonger-y.

posts.author: dev_ulmish
posts.content: From personal experience so far with AI, at least Studio one I guess it is helpful only in 2 ways: It can help you understand basic scripting concepts, by answering specific questions, which are extremely important if you wanna level up your skills. Questions like “Why is it important to use WaitForChild for workspace objects on LocalScripts”. Are answered quite understandable and more importantly you can ask it something more specifc. But it depends to the question. It may make mistakes ofc. It is awesome in congrating you when you correct it. You may ask it for a little more complex system and it would give you the least optimized system where memory leaks are must. But once you suggest it a better approach, it congrats you and gives you a better approach. Sure it would get better with time but I highly encourage you learn the basics first and then let the magic happen as you would make so much better and efficient systems than the AI itself.

posts.author: darkarai1234
posts.content: I can see how it seems a bit fearmongering ish, but I think it is something to be concerned about. You’re right in saying I didn’t actually “lose” it, but I find it similar to how doomscrolling impacts your brain. With Ai the reflexive part of your brain is highly active, so sitting and thinking about problems has a higher barrier. I’ll definitely think about how I phrase it in the future, but I do think it is something to be wary of.

posts.author: Sametc4n
posts.content: Your approach is actually solid , and you’re asking the right question at the right time. Using AI for architectural guidance without copy-pasting code forces you to understand why systems work, which is where real learning happens.Here’s the distinction that matters: What you’re doing well: - AI explaining structure and flow (quest states, progression logic, data relationships)- You’re implementing it yourself, which means you’re debugging, iterating, and internalizing the patterns- This builds intuition for larger systems faster than tutorials alone Where to draw the line: - Don’t use AI to generate entire modules you won’t read or modify- If you’re copy-pasting 70%+ of any script, you’re creating technical debt—you won’t understand it when it breaks- For monetization systems (game passes, DataStore transactions), always understand the code. These handle real money and player trust**My recommendation:**Keep doing what you’re doing, but be intentional. Use AI for:- Architecture walkthroughs (how to structure quest data, state machines, etc.)- Explaining specific problems (“why does my RemoteEvent fire twice?”)- Pseudocode and logic flowThen write the actual code yourself . Start small—maybe 50-100 lines at a time. When you hit a wall, ask AI for a specific snippet explanation, not the whole solution.By the time you’ve built 3-4 quest systems this way, you’ll naturally write them without AI help. That’s the goal. You’re not trying to become an AI operator; you’re using it as a tutor to compress your learning curve.The fact that you’re questioning yourself means you’re thinking about this correctly.

items.title: Is this Too ambitious? Tool Framework

posts.author: spookwallyjungle
posts.content: My Plan is to know the best way to do a Tool Framework in luau, with support for 3 categories, Melee, Misc and others, Melee having a global handler as the default, but all categories can be overwritten their functions, to support custom behaviors, and then make it work, so when a tool gets added to any player character, the behavior gets constructed, however if it’s already constructed, then don’t do it again, then add specific support for requiring the tools in the client or server only if specified, the tools can be constructed with tags or attributes But is this what i am trying to do too complex?, i am having a mess right now, anyone have suggestions on where to start or in what to focus first? , then what to skip And if anyone is wondering my use case, the game i am trying to do right now will contain shops, guns, and miscellaneous items like trackers, sodas, etc, i just need mostly all features so it won’t be a hassle in the future

posts.author: tristanperry
posts.content: If they are going to be be generic tools that share a large part of their functionality but need shared core features this might be a valid use case of OOP. The existing tool class tool has events* that you can piggy back off.

posts.author: mBloxxer
posts.content: If you understand how to do OOP, then you can make an inherited class list. Each unique tool has their own ModuleScript, containing information that you would like about the object. Every tool would absorb their ancestor’s functions, or have any functions overridden if defined in a more recent class. Our first class, the very beginning would be a specific weapon. For example, a “Wooden Sword”. local ancestorClass = require(MeleeClass) local class = setmetatable({}, ancestorClass) class.__index = class function class.new(root, character) local new = setmetatable(ancestorClass.new(root), class) new.root = root new.Model = script.Model:Clone() new.Damage = 15 return new end return class obviously change the ancestorClass variable to the ancestor type, such as a melee weapon or whatever you would need. Now for the first ancestorClass, a generic melee weapon class. local ancestorClass = require(BaseClass) local class = setmetatable({}, ancestorClass) class.__index = class function class.new(root) local new = setmetatable(ancestorClass.new(root), class) new.root = root return new end function class:M1() -- Weapon attack logic, such as animations, sounds, remote events, etc. end return class Now for the final and very generic Tool class. Note that all tools will eventually return to this class and so the script structure is more unique. local class = {} class.__index = class function class.new(root) local new = setmetatable({}, class) new.root = root new.__index = class return new end function class:EquipTool(tool, parent) -- Called when the tool needs to be cloned and parented to the player. end Now for explaining why and what. function class.new(root) end The new constructs the class, and will be called for when a tool is created. The base tool is structured differently as it will be the most highest parent, and so it creates the original metatable. Each child class will override or add new data and functions. The __index is used to tell the metatable that it can collect all of the functions in the class itself, so that everything is collected when invoking class.new() If you have no idea about OOP, then I can explain further. But this is my best attempt at explaining what you are trying to achieve.

posts.author: Downrest
posts.content: I know most comments would be about OOP, so let me offer an alternative approach that could be more straightforward to implement and manage. You can implement a system where ModuleScripts, intended to be different tool behaviors, have functions such as Equipped() , Unequipped() , and Activated() . Then you can have a main script that implements these functions to their respective tool events like this: -- a "behavior" module script, in this example the module is named "Sword1Behavior" local module = {} -- insert code in functions function module.Equipped() end function module.Unequipped() end function module.Activated() end return module -- main script handling the module scripts local player: Player local toolBehaviors = { ["Sword1"] = { table.unpack( require(game.ServerScriptService.Sword1Behavior); -- insert more require() calls for more behaviors ) } } player.Backpack.ChildAdded:Connect(function(tool: Tool) if tool:IsA("Tool") then local toolBehavior: {() - ()} = toolBehaviors[tool.Name] for behaviorName, behaviorFunction in toolBehaviors do pcall(function() tool[behaviorName]:Connect(behaviorFunction) end) end end end)

posts.content: I’ll try this first, I’ll let you all know how it goes, And if you can, explain more

posts.author: spookwallyjungle
posts.content: This method could also be useful, but I think it would lead to long-term, structural problems about server-client communication , Deciding if all the logic of every tool should be on the server or client is already a problem in itself but i guess that’s a problem for another time

posts.author: Beggy55
posts.content: I would recommend making some basic OOP “class” like “Item” or “CustomTool” with basic methods like GetPlayer, then have a behavior script where you can add on custom logic Item: local players = game:GetService("Players") local Item = {} Item.__index = Item function Item.new(tool: Tool, behavior: ModuleScript) local self = setmetatable({}, Item) self.Tool = tool self.Equipped = false self.Connections = {} self.Behavior = require(behavior) local function onEquipped(mouse: Mouse?) self.Equipped = true end local function onUnequipped() self.Equipped = false end table.insert( self.Connections, tool.Equipped:Connect(onEquipped) ) table.insert( self.Connections, tool.Unequipped:Connect(onUnequipped) ) if type(self.Behavior["new"]) == "function" then Behavior.new(self) end return self end --// Basic methods function Item:GetPlayer() local player = nil if self.Equipped then player = players:GetPlayerFromCharacter(self.Tool.Parent) elseif self.Tool.Parent:IsA("Backpack") then player = self.Tool.Parent.Parent end return player end return Item Behavior: local behavior = {} function behavior.new(self) --// Add extra stuff function self:Shoot() print("Shoot") end self:Shoot() end return behavior That way you can have basic shared functionality (Like when the tool is equipped, make self.Equipped = true), then you can add on custom stuff. For your system, just have 3 behaviors that are meant for their own stuff, like having some general behavior script for a melee weapon.

posts.author: mBloxxer
posts.content: I would make a script to setup your classes for you. for _, module in replicatedFirst.Items:QueryDescendants("ModuleScript") do get.Items[module.Name] = require(module) end This one grabs every module script and registers it into the root. image 309×262 8.4 KB This is how I structure my classes. You do your weapon modules like this -- Sword return function(self) self.Damage = 25 warn("Sword behavior enabled") function self:M1() print("Sword m1") end end But I recommend to make it a module script that returns a table, not a function. A table can contain all of the important data without creating several new instances of that data that already exists. If you had organized your code into something like: -- Default Melee local class = setmetatable({}, baseClass) class.__index = class function weaponClass.new() local new = setmetatable({}, class) new.Damage = 25 return new end function weaponClass:M1() end function weaponClass:OnEquip() end Leaving the M1 function inside of the Sword class instead of the DefaultMelee class defeats the purpose of making your code inherited. Also the other replies are a bit of misdirection as they aren’t organized like how I explained. All of the classes I showed can be handled both on the client and server, with just specifying on each item type. Default Melee Class: function class:MouseButton1Server or function class:MouseButton1Client

posts.author: Beggy55
posts.content: You’re doing pretty good, just have a system in place for sub-behaviors Basically you can have generic behaviors like “Gun”, then have a sub behavior for “FullAutoGun”, Thatway you can add on to, or make new functions for the BaseTool(ex: making a shoot function, and do some generic stuff like make self.Shooting = true), then in the full auto sub-behavior, you can change how the shoot function behaves even more by adding on to it I would also recommend for your tools using this framework to have this setup A Tool (Obviously) Then under that tool, a configuration instance (So you can store data for your framework, like making ammo = 10, and maxammo = 10, and making reload time to 3 seconds) Then optionally a model, most advanced frameworks I’ve seen use this, Basically, instead of messing around with the old legacy Handle system for tools, we use models, you will have to manually weld or motor6d them to a character. (Make sure RequireHandle is set to false on your tool if you aren’t using Handles) This will also allow you do make the model of the tool appear on the character whenever it’s “Grabbed”, let me know if you need help I this I can provide some code showing on how to detect when the tool is “Grabbed” or “Dropped”, and then to attach the tool to the character of the tool.

"..tool.Name) local tags = CollectionService:GetTags(tool) warn("tags for "..tool.Name) warn(tags) local config for _, tag in tags do warn(tag) if Registry[tag] then config = Registry[tag] break end end warn(config) if not config then warn("No specific config found, using default") config = Registry.Default end local class = Classes[config.Class] if not class then return end local object = class.new(tool) -- default behavior if config.DefaultBehavior then local behavior = Behaviors[config.DefaultBehavior] if behavior then ApplyBehavior(object, behavior) end end -- override behavior if config.Behavior then local behavior = Behaviors[config.Behavior] if behavior then ApplyBehavior(object, behavior) end end Constructed[tool] = object end CollectionService:GetInstanceAddedSignal("Tool"):Connect(construct) print("Tool Initialized") -- Handle existing tools --for _, tool in CollectionService:GetTagged("Tool") do -- construct(tool) --end Behaviors DefaultMelee : local DefaultMelee = { Damage = 10 } function DefaultMelee:OnEquip() print("Equipped Default Melee", self.Tool) warn(self) end return DefaultMelee

posts.author: mBloxxer
posts.content: Exactly! And things like function Sword:M1() can actually be moved to the Default Melee class, unless if you wanted different functionality for specifically that sword class. The way how it works is it first goes trough every parent class, for your case it would start at Sword → DefaultMelee → DefaultTool, and it pulls all of the functions and data from each parent class. Anything named the same will override the existing function/data. As for my script that has get in it, get is just a variable that contains all of the module scripts that will be applied to the root of my code. OOP is a bit tricky in lua but you can figure it out with a handful of devforum posts and even the lua documentation itself provides nice explanations. You can think of OOP as a center point for all of your code being the root, and in the initialization of all of your modules it goes through each script and sets up its constructor to be added to the root. So then all scripts are connected and you can call events through each module script, for example: -- Initialization module local get = {} get.Modules = {} function initialization.Begin() local root = {} root.InputModule = get.Modules.Input.new(root) end -- Input Module local class = {} class.__index = class function class.new(root) local new = setmetatable({}, class) new.root = root return new end return class Initialization will be called by a script in the StarterPlayerScripts area. Note that in my code examples, the variable names are not something you have to follow it’s just how I format my code. A lot of people will use self in place of where I type new . self is simply just how the code passes ‘itself’ around, which is why you use the __index . __index Allows for the class to interact with other variables using a colon operator. The colon operator is what passes the self argument, if you called functions with a dot operator then self is not passed by default and would have to be it’s own parameter. Kinda complicated to understand at first but the more you work with it and more you get better at scripting the easier it gets. I don’t recommend doing every project in OOP, but it’s perfect for the long term development cycle of games.

items.title: How do I Learn How to Script?

posts.author: ItsmeBeetle
posts.content: This is my first topic so this may be the wrong category, so please excuse me. I want to learn scripting Luau. I understand that publishing games on Roblox is hard now, and I do not have a form of getting any Robux. I only have 167 robux, and I know that you need 1,000 robux to publish anything. Should I start by reading the documentation? Watching YouTube tutorials? Learning the syntax of Luau? I don’t know where to start. I already have basic scripting knowledge down, like printing, basic functions, basic if else statements, learning a bit of syntax, basic loops, and am now learning how to require() from module scripts, and using Server scripts, module scripts, and local scripts for the right things. It seems like I am in a good place right now… but I need to expand my knowledge. If anyone could see this (which is quite rare), and at least gave it a reply or like, I would very much appreciate it. Thank you for reading!

posts.author: Godzilla_RealLife
posts.content: your real programming knowledge will expand through trial and error and problem solving so if you want to learn more advanced things look up roblox documentation for accuracy, get help from the community through devforum although watching youtube tutorials may introduce outdated code, copy and pasted code and bad code patterns if you watch a youtube tutorial that was 2-8 years ago so you should learn from them if they were created atleast maximum 1 year ago in my opinion

posts.author: 2112Jay
posts.content: Where did you find that you need 1,000 to publish a game? You need 0. Best way to learn how to script is to script.. AlvinBlox on YouTube covers almost everything a starting programmer could ever hope to know. Super solid start.

posts.author: ItsmeBeetle
posts.content: bro have you seen the news???

posts.author: Chylled0
posts.content: Using ai to learn it great, have the ai create something for your game, ask it to explain how the code works, then recode it. When you discover something new just go over it in the roblox docs. I didn’t really use ai cause it sucked at the time, and whilst I can create better code than ai it’s finally able to make working stuff. Honestly how I learned was recreating something I definitely cannot make, and recoding it over and over again until it worked. Also about the 1000 robux thing, it’s not an issue at all, you can for sure make 1000 robux quickly from a simple cashgrab game. You only need 1000 robux to publish a under age 16 game, makes sense.

posts.author: racecarbo
posts.content: I recommend using YouTube tutorials to help you learn Roblox scripting, that’s the way I learnt how to code. Personally, I think you should use the BrawlDev beginner scripting playlist.

posts.author: JebbGames
posts.content: I strongly agree that trial and error is great- but don’t get stuck in the trap of “Tutorial Hell”. You’ve probably heard it before, I recommend watching a few tutorials and then just trying things out. Use documentation or dev forum if you get stuck. Make finished products. Seriously, this is a big one. Motivation is great, but discipline is better. AI is also good, but you should be just as good without it as with it. Use it as a tool to ship things faster rather than to build things for you. As for the Robux thing, 1000 Robux is basically nothing- you can do commissions once you get better and are actually ready to publish games. TLDR, just practice. It sounds painful, but it’s the only way to get better at anything.

posts.author: ig1oo1
posts.content: Perhaps you can try something like this: local RunService = game:GetService("RunService") local camera = workspace.CurrentCamera local distortionIntensity = 0.2 local duration = 1 RunService.RenderStepped:Connect(function() local distortionFactor = math.sin(workspace.DistributedGameTime * math.pi / duration) * distortionIntensity local distortionMatrix = CFrame.new( 0, 0, 0, 1, distortionFactor, 0, distortionFactor, 1, 0, 0, 0, 1 ) camera.CFrame = camera.CFrame * distortionMatrix end)

posts.content: I managed to get this using 200 viewport frames clipped by 200 normal frames, which is obviously not very performant. I also had a lot of trouble rendering the sky, so I just settled with a black background. I would stick with my original post, just because it is far more performant - you could add some kind of transition (like the black fade shown in your video) and it would look decent. EDIT: Here's the code (lighting/skybox cloning is on line 56) local Players = game:GetService("Players") local RunService = game:GetService("RunService") local Lighting = game:GetService("Lighting") local player = Players.LocalPlayer local playerGui = player:WaitForChild("PlayerGui") local camera = workspace.CurrentCamera local NUM_STRIPES = 200 local WARP_SPEED = 2 local WARP_AMPLITUDE = 25 local WARP_FREQUENCY = 3.5 local gui = Instance.new("ScreenGui") gui.IgnoreGuiInset = true gui.Parent = playerGui local stripes = {} for i = 1, NUM_STRIPES do local clip = Instance.new("Frame") clip.BackgroundTransparency = 0 clip.BackgroundColor3 = Color3.fromRGB(0, 0, 0) clip.ClipsDescendants = true clip.BorderSizePixel = 0 clip.ZIndex = 1 clip.Parent = gui local vp = Instance.new("ViewportFrame") vp.BackgroundTransparency = 1 vp.BackgroundColor3 = Color3.fromRGB(0, 0, 0) vp.LightColor = Color3.fromRGB(255, 255, 255) vp.LightDirection = Vector3.new(-1, -2, -1) vp.Ambient = Color3.fromRGB(90, 90, 90) vp.ZIndex = 1 vp.Parent = clip local worldModel = Instance.new("WorldModel") worldModel.Parent = vp local vpCam = Instance.new("Camera") vpCam.Parent = vp vp.CurrentCamera = vpCam stripes[i] = { clip = clip, vp = vp, worldModel = worldModel, vpCam = vpCam } end local function capture(maxDistance) maxDistance = maxDistance or 200 local camCFrame = camera.CFrame local camFOV = camera.FieldOfView local camPos = camCFrame.Position -- doesnt work lmao -- this is probably the largest area of improvment local skyObjects = {} for _, obj in Lighting:GetChildren() do if obj:IsA("Sky") or obj:IsA("Atmosphere") or obj:IsA("BloomEffect") or obj:IsA("ColorCorrectionEffect") or obj:IsA("SunRaysEffect") or obj:IsA("BlurEffect") or obj:IsA("DepthOfFieldEffect") then table.insert(skyObjects, obj) end end local toClone = {} for _, obj in workspace:GetChildren() do if not (obj:IsA("BasePart") or obj:IsA("Model")) then continue end if obj:IsDescendantOf(playerGui) then continue end if obj:IsA("Terrain") then continue end local pos if obj:IsA("BasePart") then pos = obj.Position elseif obj:IsA("Model") then if obj.PrimaryPart then pos = obj.PrimaryPart.Position else for _, d in obj:GetDescendants() do if d:IsA("BasePart") then pos = d.Position break end end end end if pos and (pos - camPos).Magnitude = maxDistance then table.insert(toClone, obj) end end for _, stripe in stripes do stripe.worldModel:ClearAllChildren() stripe.vpCam.CFrame = camCFrame stripe.vpCam.FieldOfView = camFOV for _, obj in skyObjects do obj:Clone().Parent = stripe.worldModel end for _, obj in toClone do obj.Archivable = true lo

posts.author: Ionypex23
posts.content: The viewport frame approach is a massive performance hit, but it is probably the only way to get that specific distortion without actual shaders. The stripping logic is clever, though the cloning process for parts and the skybox is definitely going to scale poorly in a larger workspace.

posts.author: ig1oo1
posts.content: hello: I managed to get this using 200 viewport frames clipped by 200 normal frames, which is obviously not very performant. Thank you for mentioning what I’ve mentioned. Perhaps only adding parts that are in the player’s view and less than a certain distance away would greatly increase the performance.

posts.author: Ionypex23
posts.content: Limiting the distance and view frustum is a good start, but cloning everything every frame is still going to be a bottleneck regardless of distance. You might want to look into using a pool of parts or just updating the existing ones instead of clearing and re-cloning the whole world model every time.

posts.author: ig1oo1
posts.content: It’s not every frame. It’s essentially taking a screenshot of the scene and warping that. As far as I could tell, the OP does not need it to constantly update.

posts.author: Ionypex23
posts.content: Even if it is just a one-time capture, the cloning overhead for 200 stripes is still heavy. If the scene is complex, that ClearAllChildren and the subsequent loop through the workspace is going to cause a noticeable hitch regardless of how often it runs.

posts.author: Ionypex23
posts.content: Culling by distance helps, but the real bottleneck is still the sheer number of clones and the overhead of 200 ViewportFrames. Even with distance checks, you’re still doing a massive amount of work every frame just to keep the stripes updated.

posts.author: ig1oo1
posts.content: Are you sure changing the frame and viewport size and position is a massive amount of work?

posts.author: Ionypex23
posts.content: The property updates themselves aren’t the issue, it’s the sheer number of them. Moving 200 UI elements and 200 ViewportFrames every single frame via RenderStepped is still a lot of overhead for the engine to manage, especially when you consider the layout recalculations that come with changing sizes and positions.

posts.author: superlivia10alt
posts.content: Heya! I’m not sure what you mean but you could try something like Humanoid.PlatformStand = true (this doesn’t make the limbs flap around but it does make them fall). If you want the limbs to flap around aswell but sometimes play animations youd need to not break motor6d’s and instead disable them using Motor6d.Enabled and then furthermore do something similar as to Animator.AnimationPlayed:Connect(function(Track) if IsRagdolled then -- Enable all the motor 6ds task.wait(Track.Length) -- Disable all the motor 6ds end end) Hope this helped​:victory_hand:

posts.author: sweetpuppy
posts.content: Active Ragdolls are sick! It’s basically the holy grail of physics-based games. To get that ‘holding themselves’ vibe, you’ll probably want to look into AlignOrientation or ConfigurableJoints . The trick is keeping the ragdoll limbs ‘stiff’ enough to follow an animation track while still reacting to physics. You’re basically syncing a hidden animated character with the physical ragdoll parts using high-torque constraints. And yeah, doing it in a ModuleScript is actually the best way to go. It makes the code way cleaner and easier for other people to use once you go open source. You can just have a Module.Enable(character) function that handles the joint swapping and constraint logic. Good luck with it! If you pull it off, the community is gonna love that open-source release.

posts.author: Sametc4n
posts.content: Your code is actually pretty close! The main issue is likely where you’re placing this script and how you’re handling the spinning mechanic . Here’s what you need to fix:**Key Problems:**1. LocalScript placement — This needs to be in StarterPlayer StarterPlayerScripts or StarterGui , not in ServerScriptService. LocalScripts can only run on the client.2. Physics setup — AngularVelocity requires an Attachment parented to the part you want to spin. Your code does this, but you’re destroying it too fast (0.6s might feel jarring). Try 0.8–1.2 seconds instead for smoother feel.3. Missing debounce — Without debounce, spam-pressing E will create multiple spin instances. Add this: lualocal debounce = falseUIS.InputBegan:Connect(function(input, processed) if processed or debounce then return end if input.KeyCode == Enum.KeyCode.E then debounce = true local char = player.Character if not char then debounce = false return end local root = char:FindFirstChild("HumanoidRootPart") local hum = char:FindFirstChild("Humanoid") if root and hum and hum.Health 0 then hum:SetStateEnabled(Enum.HumanoidStateType.Climbing, false) hum.Jump = true local attachment = Instance.new("Attachment", root) local spin = Instance.new("AngularVelocity") spin.Attachment0 = attachment spin.AngularVelocity = Vector3.new(0, 25, 0) spin.MaxTorque = 100000 spin.Parent = root task.wait(1) spin:Destroy() attachment:Destroy() hum:SetStateEnabled(Enum.HumanoidStateType.Climbing, true) end debounce = false endend) What changed: - Added debounce flag- Increased MaxTorque and AngularVelocity for more noticeable spin- Disabled climbing state so players can’t stick to walls mid-spin- Check hum.Health 0 to prevent errorsTest this and adjust the `Angular

posts.author: panxi0n
posts.content: Your issue mainly comes from how you’re handling CFrame and the weld. You are forcing the target position with self.TargetLimb.CFrame = self.AttackerLimb.CFrame . This completely breaks your offset. You should not set the CFrame manually before creating the weld. Also, your offset is not applied in the correct reference frame. It needs to be relative to the attacker’s limb, not in world space. You should use a Motor6D , which is more stable for this kind of system. Do not set the CFrame manually anymore. The Motor6D will handle positioning with the correct offset.
