items.title: Vanguard - A Modular Framework for Roblox Developers

posts.author: risewrd
posts.content: vanguard-banner 1774×887 54 KB Vanguard is a modern, modular Roblox framework built for developers who want a scalable and maintainable foundation for their experiences. Inspired by the strengths of Knit while modernizing the development experience, Vanguard provides a familiar workflow through Services and Controllers while introducing a more flexible architecture, structured lifecycle system, built-in networking primitives, and type-friendly APIs. Whether you’re developing a solo project or a large-scale experience, Vanguard helps keep your code organized, maintainable, and easy to expand. rise: Vanguard 0.1.14 is now available Links Vanguard Documentation Vanguard Source Code Vanguard On Wally Availability Notice Vanguard is currently available through Wally only . A standalone Roblox Toolbox package is planned for a future release. If you would like to inspect the framework, contribute to development, or manually install it, the complete source code is available on GitHub. Current Installation Methods Wally Package Source Code via GitHub Roblox Toolbox Package (Coming Soon) Why Vanguard? Knit has been a staple of Roblox development for years, but many projects have outgrown its original design. Vanguard was created to provide a familiar development experience while modernizing the framework architecture. Familiar Workflow Developers coming from Knit will find familiar concepts such as Services and Controllers while benefiting from an improved startup system and modern APIs. Modular by Design Vanguard encourages clean project organization and separation of concerns from the start. Built for Scale Whether you’re working alone or with a team, Vanguard provides a foundation that grows alongside your project. Developer Focused Vanguard prioritizes maintainability, readability, documentation, and long-term project health. Features Service Architecture Controller Architecture Promise-Based Startup Structured Lifecycle Hooks Automatic Module Registration Built-In Networking Layer Remote Methods Reliable Signals Replicated Properties Components System Logging System Dependency-Free Core Rojo Support Wally Support Type-Friendly APIs Familiar Knit-Inspired Workflow Designed for Scalability Installation Requirements Roblox Studio A Rojo Project Wally 0.3.x Vanguard 0.1.14 Vanguard is dependency-free. All utility modules are included directly in the package. Install with Wally Add Vanguard to your project’s wally.toml : [dependencies] Vanguard = "twrblxdevs/vanguard@0.1.14" Install dependencies: wally install Map the generated Packages directory into ReplicatedStorage: { "name": "MyGame", "tree": { "$className": "DataModel", "ReplicatedStorage": { "Packages": { "$path": "Packages" } } } } Require Vanguard: local ReplicatedStorage = game:GetService("ReplicatedStorage") local Vanguard = require(ReplicatedStorage.Packages.Vanguard) Quick Start Recommended Project Layout src ├─ server │ ├─ Bootstrapper.server.luau │ └─ Services │ └─ GreetingService.luau ├─ cl

posts.author: risewrd
posts.content: That’s a fair question. A provider-based structure is absolutely valid, and I agree with many of the points in Sleitnick’s post. I don’t think services/controllers are universally better or that providers are inherently unsafe. Vanguard keeps the distinction because Roblox has a meaningful client/server trust boundary. Services represent server-owned state, authorization, networking, and game logic, while controllers represent client-owned input, UI, and presentation. That lets Vanguard provide predictable lifecycle ordering, clearer types, automatic networking behavior, and validation around where code is allowed to run. The safety benefits also are not only for beginners. Explicit architectural boundaries can reduce ambiguity in larger projects and make unfamiliar code easier to navigate. A completely generalized provider system offers more freedom, but each project then has to establish and enforce those conventions itself. That said, services and controllers should usually be thin composition roots. Reusable domain logic can still live in ordinary modules, registered classes, components, and utilities rather than being forced into one large service or controller. I’m also open to supporting a generalized provider API alongside services and controllers if there is enough demand. The goal is not to claim that providers are wrong, but to give Vanguard a predictable default that fits Roblox networking and security while still allowing modular code.

items.title: Avatar Joint Upgrade (AJU) Phase 2 Rollout & Updated Migration Recommendations

posts.author: Homeomorph
posts.content: Key Takeaways Avatar Joint Upgrade Phase 2 is now rolling out. This replaces traditional Motor6D joints with physics-capable constraints to provide better character movement. You can temporarily disable it in StarterPlayer if you encounter breaking changes. Hi Creators, We’re transitioning to Phase 2 of the Avatar Joint Upgrade (AJU) rollout , which means that all games with the StarterPlayer.AvatarJointUpgrade property set to ‘Default’ will now automatically receive the new logic which replaces traditional Motor6D joints with physics-capable AnimationConstraints and BallSocketConstraints meant to provide better movement capabilities and enable higher-fidelity avatars. If your game encounters any breaking changes or unexpected behavior due to this engine update, you can temporarily opt out by setting StarterPlayer.AvatarJointUpgrade to Disabled and republishing. Note that this is only a temporary safety net. We’re targeting Phase 3 (full, forceful migration for all games) by the end of the year. If you have to disable the upgrade, please leave a comment on this thread explaining exactly what broke so our team can actively help you troubleshoot and migrate your codebase before the final forced migration. Updated Migration Recommendations As we transition into Phase 2, we have an updated recommendation for script-driven character animations. 1. Do NOT modify RigAttachment.CFrame or Attachment0.CFrame for procedural Animations. In our previous posts, we initially suggested that developers looking to migrate away from Motor6D.C0/C1 logic could do a find-and-replace to modify RigAttachment.CFrame instead. While this can work for reading C0/C1, we don’t recommend using this for procedural animations. Animation Retargeting depends on RigAttachments to adapt animations to different body proportions. Constantly moving RigAttachments can cause performance problems and bad Retargeting behavior. 2. Our Recommended Alternative: Modify .Transform Locally during PreSimulation If you’re procedurally animating character joints (such as implementing custom torso twisting, weapon recoil, or slope-alignment), we recommend multiplying the AnimationConstraint.Transform property during the RunService.PreSimulation event. Because .Transform is updated locally by the animation compositor and reset every frame, this approach layers your procedural math perfectly on top of active animation tracks without disrupting retargeting. Example Implementation: -- Before local originalC0 = neck.C0 RunService.RenderStepped:Connect(function() neck.C0 = originalC0 * computeNeckRotation() end) -- After RunService.PreSimulation:Connect(function() -- run after Animation applies the Transform if not animator.EvaluationThrottled then -- skip this frame if Animation is throttled by LOD neck.Transform = computeNeckRotation() * neck.Transform end end) 3. Avoid using Attachment.CFrame to replicate procedural Animations from the Server Some developers preferred setting C0 on the server so it rep

items.title: [Knit] InputController | Strictly Typed, Cross-Platform & Memory-Safe

posts.author: chomik9991
posts.content: Hey everyone! If you’ve ever built a game using Knit, you know that handling inputs cleanly across Keyboard, Gamepad, and Mobile can quickly messy, and cause memory leaks or race condictions Thats why I built this open source InputController , a strictly typed, Promise-driven, and memory-safe input manager for Knit. I’m releasing it open-source for anyone who wants a clean foundation for their game’s controls. Key Features True Cross-Platform Support: Bind an action once, and it automatically listens for Keyboard, Gamepad, and on-screen Touch GUI buttons simultaneously. Memory-Safe by Default: Powered by Trove , every input connection (especially tricky Touch UI events) is automatically tracked and cleaned up when you unbind an action. No more ghost inputs or memory leaks. Zero Yielding (Promise-Driven): Mobile UI loading is handled asynchronously using Promise . If a script tries to bind a touch control before the UI exists, the controller queues it safely in the background instead of freezing your framework with WaitForChild . Strictly Typed: Written with --!strict Luau. You get full autocomplete for all your bindings, keymaps, and methods. How it works Binding an action is simple. You just provide an action name, a dictionary of the inputs you want to listen to, and your Down / Up callback functions. function MyController:KnitStart() local InputController = Knit.GetController("InputController") -- Bind a unified action for Sprinting InputController:BindAction( "Sprint", { Keyboard = Enum.KeyCode.LeftShift, Gamepad = Enum.KeyCode.ButtonL3, Touch = true -- Automatically hooks up to a "Sprint" GuiButton! }, function() print("Sprint Started!") end, function() print("Sprint Ended!") end ) end Unbinding is just as easy, and automatically cleans up all associated connections and Troves: InputController:UnBindAction("Sprint") Installation Get the model from Roblox’s asset store. Drag and drop it where your Controllers folder is, all done. Configuration If you want you can change the location of your Mobile Gui template inside the file where MOBILE_GUI_PATH is. Adding buttons When a bind is created and Touch == true then the script searches the mobileGui for a button with the same unique name as name of the bind Dependencies: This controller relies on a few standard open-source packages: Knit Promise Trove Input Ps. Knit Automaticly comes with this packages included. Get the Module: InputController Download InputController GitHub Page Why I built this A lot of input controllers either ignore mobile support entirely or force you to use messy WaitForChild loops to make sure the PlayerGui is loaded. I wanted a senior-level architecture where controllers can register their intents instantly, and the framework handles the timing behind the scenes. Its my first post and a first public resource i made, so let me know what do you think, or if you have any features, ideas! also i would apreciate reporting any bugs you find. thanks!! Happy coding, vynxz

items.title: Introducing the US 18+ DevEx Rate: Earn 42% More on Spend from 18+ US Players

posts.author: peraldon
posts.content: Key Takeaways Today, we’re announcing an initiative to reward novel game development: a 42% increase in the DevEx rate for spend by U.S. players 18 and older in eligible games ( see more on eligibility below ) starting June 8. We’re also giving you the tools and analytics to track your earnings by the specific rate. If you’re building games for an older audience, there has never been a better time to build on Roblox. [Update] June 8, 2026 peraldon: The US 18+ DevEx rate is live. Starting today, eligible in-game spend from age-checked 18+ US players cashes out at $0.54 per 100 Earned Robux, a 42% increase over the standard rate. If your game is eligible, you’re earning at the higher rate starting today. Check whether your game is eligible in the Creator Dashboard . [Update] May 29, 2026 peraldon: Hi Creators, We want to share full details on which games qualify for the US DevEx 18+ rate . This includes complete details on custom avatar systems and games without avatars. We know from research that many 18+ players who aren’t currently on Roblox are drawn in by high fidelity visuals that signal the games they’re looking for are here. For avatars, high fidelity means a high degree of articulation with more joints and greater range of motion. The US 18+ DevEx rate is designed to reward games built for the audience looking for exactly that. Your game is eligible if any of the following apply: Avatars are always R15. Avatars are always a custom human-form rig with 15 or more joints or parts distributed across the body in a human-like way. Avatars are always a custom non-human-form rig (quadrupeds, dragons, vehicles, anything that isn’t bipedal). There are no visible player characters (i.e. puzzle games, strategy games, and card games). To qualify, Avatar Settings must be set to R15 Only. If you’re using a custom avatar system, make sure your setup meets the requirements above and the intent behind them. If it does, your game automatically qualifies at launch. We’ll review on an ongoing basis and may remove eligibility for games that don’t qualify. Eventually, we’ll ask you to formally declare that your custom avatar setup qualifies. Full details, examples, group payout info, and FAQs are live on Creator Hub. Review now before the rate goes live on June 8. Why We’re Doing This The 18 to 34 user cohort on Roblox is growing over 50% year-over-year in the U.S.—and monetizes 50% higher than the under 18 cohort. We’re rewarding creators who build the genres, gameplay mechanics, and visual styles that appeal to this audience by increasing the DevEx rate by 42% for spend from age-checked 18 and older U.S. players in eligible games. 1. Based on Q1 2026 data. For further information, please see the Special Note Regarding Age-Check included in our Shareholder Letter, dated April 30, 2026 available on ir.roblox.com What You Need to Know Starting June 8, 2026 , we’re increasing DevEx for a specific category of spend by 42% : Category of Spend Current Rate New Rate E

items.title: SafeChat v1.0 — Button-Based Chat System (No Free Text)

posts.author: Henrycooper9
posts.content: SafeChat is a modular, registry-based chat menu system that replaces free-text chat with a clean, structured button UI — fully integrated with TextChatService . Perfect for: Roleplay games Console/mobile-friendly experiences Moderation-heavy systems IMPORTANT : This is an early version of the package i am not ui artist i tried my best Demo Screenshot 2026-05-06 175308 717×663 88 KB Installation Get it Here (Package) OR -Download Place Here Features Button-based chat (no typing required) Modular architecture (Core + UI + Config) Registry system (no more huge tables passed around) Nested submenus (clean multi-level UI) Dynamic runtime injection (add menus anytime) Strict validation (prevents UI crashes) Mobile-friendly layout Quick Start Place SafeChat in ReplicatedStorage Require it from a LocalScript: local SafeChat = require(game.ReplicatedStorage.SafeChat) SafeChat.Open() Registry System (Main Concept) SafeChat uses a Registry instead of passing UI data manually. SafeChat.New(data) -- Add menu items SafeChat.Clear() -- Clear all menus SafeChat.Get() -- Get current menu data Menu Types Types.Leaf("Hello") -- Sends message Types.Submenu("Actions", {...}) -- Opens submenu Types.Header("Roleplay") -- Section label Types.Divider() -- Separator Types.Toggle("Music", true, callback) Types.Icon("Wave", imageId) Example local SafeChat = require(game.ReplicatedStorage.SafeChat) local Types = require(SafeChat.Core.Types) SafeChat.New({ Kind = "Submenu", Name = "Teleport", Options = { Types.Leaf("Lobby"), Types.Leaf("Arena") } }) Technical Notes Uses TextChatService:SendAsync() ( RBXGeneral ) UI auto-closes on selection Data validation via Types.Validate() Feedback I’d love to hear your thoughts, suggestions, or features you’d like to see! i would love to keep updating this package Contributors @Henrycooper9 @ForeverHD for the package TopbarPlus

posts.content: Discord Webhook Proxy: Your Webhooks, Turbocharged. discord.yeesen.dev Discord Webhook Proxy A Secure, Self-Healing Proxy for Discord Webhooks. Stop getting rate-limited and start delivering. If you’ve ever had your Discord webhooks fail because of traffic spikes, or hit a wall because of Discord’s restrictions on Roblox API communication, you know the frustration of broken data pipelines. Meet the Discord Webhook Proxy - a robust, high-performance solution designed to bridge the gap between your applications and Discord, regardless of your platform. The Roblox-Discord Bridge We’ve engineered this proxy to be the ultimate solution for Roblox developers. By acting as an intermediary, this proxy effectively bypasses the blocks that typically prevent direct communication between Roblox and Discord, allowing you to seamlessly connect your game data to your Discord server. Whether you are sending logs, player stats, or real-time updates, your game can now communicate with Discord effortlessly. Transform Your Webhook URL Instantly Ready to upgrade? Just swap the beginning of your current Discord webhook URL to point to our proxy. It’s a seamless, drop-in replacement that takes seconds to set up: Original: https://discord.com/api/webhooks/WEBHOOK_ID/WEBHOOK_TOKEN Proxy Endpoint: https://discord.yeesen.dev/api/webhooks/WEBHOOK_ID/WEBHOOK_TOKEN Why This Proxy Is a Game-Changer Unlike basic, bare-bones proxies, this engine is built for massive scale and reliability. It is designed to be the fastest and most resilient solution available on the DevForum. Global Performance: We aren’t just hosting in one place. Your requests are processed across four global regions (US West, US East, EU West, and Southeast Asia), ensuring that your webhooks hit Discord with lightning-fast speeds—averaging below 20ms per request. Intelligent Queue Management: If you blast thousands of requests, this proxy won’t choke. It utilizes a sophisticated Redis-backed queue system that manages your traffic efficiently, ensuring every single webhook reaches its destination without dropping. Smart Retry Logic: Don’t let a temporary 429 Rate Limit stop you. Our proxy automatically detects Discord’s rate limits and intelligently reschedules failed jobs with an exponential backoff strategy, guaranteeing delivery even during high-load scenarios. Replica-Safe Architecture: Designed for scale, the system supports multiple concurrent replicas. Whether you’re running it on Railway or your own infrastructure, the system stays synchronized and reliable across distributed instances. Open Source Built for You Transparency is key. This project is fully open-source , meaning you can see exactly how your data is handled. While we offer a hosted version for your convenience, you have the total freedom to self-host it on your own hardware. github.com GitHub - dqlistic/Discord-Webhook-Proxy Contribute to dqlistic/Discord-Webhook-Proxy development by creating an account on GitHub. Note: To host this yoursel

posts.content: Blah: Why do you need a proxy? Discord has blocked some of the roblox servers (not AWS ones), And it will not post for those servers.

posts.author: dqlistic
posts.content: Discord completely blocks direct HTTP traffic from Roblox servers with a 403 by blacklisting Roblox’s default User-Agent and server IP ranges. Even if that wasn’t the case, direct webhook link is a fragile fire-and-forget method that permanently drops your data the moment something triggers a rate limit, sudden failure, etc. My proxy turns this into a guaranteed transaction layer by buffering those sudden traffic bursts into a durable Redis FIFO queue, which dynamically parses Discord’s ‘Retry-After’ headers to execute exponential backoffs, and rescheduling jobs until they are successfully delivered.

posts.content: This is a great tool, I have my own private proxy. But making a tool like this public is just going to enable the abusers, the ones that are the reason discord blocks roblox. Discord doesn’t block roblox for fun or because they hate them. They do it becomes people kept abusing it, spamming the webhooks with, for example, chat messages.. Proxies for discord webhooks existed in the past on the devforum, but they were all eventually taken down by their owner. I’m not sure why, but likely the same reason or similar

posts.author: spookwallyjungle
posts.content: I really hope people don’t abuse this, It’s so useful to me

posts.author: dqlistic
posts.content: Valid concern, but past public proxies failed because they blindly forwarded everything. This system uses Redis to intercept traffic at the gateway and drops abusive requests before they ever reach Discord. If a script tries to flood my endpoint, the proxy issues an automatic block where the cooldown duration scales exponentially based on the violation count and burst size. I am confident that my proxy will isolate bad actors as it completely absorbs and kills spam loops at the proxy layer to keep it entirely off Discord’s radar.

items.title: Official Roblox Developer Certifications

posts.author: Velphion
posts.content: I honestly think Roblox should introduce official developer certifications/licenses that can be linked directly to platforms like LinkedIn and resumes, similar to GitHub certifications. Right now, Roblox developers are massively underestimated outside the platform, even though many are building real large-scale systems involving networking, optimization, backend architecture, monetization, live operations, and security. A proper certification system would not only help developers, but would also massively benefit Roblox as a company. It would: increase the credibility of the Roblox ecosystem, attract more serious developers and studios from all around the IT and game development industry, create clearer hiring standards, improve overall game quality, encourage developers to learn best practices, and help position Roblox as an actual technology/game development platform instead of “just a kids game.” It would also make Roblox seem like a more serious company to invest both time and money into. Verified certifications could also help reduce scams, fake developers, and toxicity in the Roblox development community. Right now, it’s often difficult to tell who is actually experienced and trustworthy versus who is simply overclaiming their abilities. A verification/reputation system would help developers, teams, and studios quickly identify: trusted developers, verified experience, real technical knowledge, and proven production history. Examples of certifications could include: Luau scripting Networking replication Optimization/performance Backend/data systems Security anti-exploit UI/UX systems Multiplayer architecture And advanced verified achievements tied to real production experience: Worked on a game with 10k+/50k+/100k+ CCU Maintained a large-scale live game Verified UGC creator Verified technical artist These certifications should not just be “watch videos and answer quizzes.” They should involve practical timed exams, real-world engineering challenges, and verification of production-level knowledge. I genuinely think a system like this could strengthen Roblox’s reputation in both the tech and gaming industry while also giving developers more recognition for the skills they build on the platform. (AI assisted writing, original concept by humans.)

posts.author: Reditect
posts.content: Too many certification examples imo. It’d be better to have a tier system, say beginner, intermediate, advanced certs like Unity. Yet, Roblox isn’t sought in the game industry like engine competitors. Their reputation for a kids platform, yes, but also the safety issues, poor creator revenue split, and not acting on creator sentiment hurts them. Made worse by recent changes to publishing requirements without the creator split changed. None of those are changing anytime soon for the game industry to reconsider it’s view of Roblox. And, even then, I got the advice from a producer within the game industry that certifications are rarely looked at by employers. The investment -usually requiring a fee and lots of time- isn’t worth what you get in return, even off Roblox. I can hear the thing about seeing developer to developer understanding of their skill levels though.

posts.author: Velphion
posts.content: That’s honestly fair criticism, especially regarding certifications not being heavily valued compared to portfolios and shipped projects. I also agree that a simpler tier-based system would probably make more sense internally within the Roblox ecosystem. Where I slightly disagree though is on the reputation side. I don’t think certifications alone would suddenly make the broader game industry fully respect Roblox , but I do think increasing Roblox’s visibility in more professional environments like LinkedIn could slowly improve its reputation and the reputation of its developers over time. At the end of the day, Roblox is home to some of the highest CCU games in gaming history, and many developers on the platform are working on genuinely large-scale systems involving networking, live operations, monetization, optimization, and backend infrastructure. I also think Roblox developers themselves could benefit from recognized certifications later on when applying for jobs outside of Roblox’s ecosystem. Even if employers still mainly care about portfolios and shipped work, certifications could still help demonstrate experience and make Roblox development appear more legitimate to outsiders unfamiliar with the platform.

posts.author: paswa
posts.content: Personally, I don’t think this is required. If you work for a game company, and it’s fairly established, then it contains an LLC + a LinkedIn page. Currently, the game I work for has that, and I am throwing that LLC and my position title directly onto my resume, and it works just fine. You can always list a project on your resume and explain what you did precisely . A certification is fairly broad and would probably be very ineffective the more it’s used. Roblox, while it’s a game platform, handles around 90% of the things internally for you, so it’s not like you are managing a live-service game 100%. Just a small portion of the live-service game is what you control and manage. Lets also assume the following: You get your application reviewed. You have a roblox certification in anit-cheat, backend/data systems, and networking and replication. Well… Sadly that’s all for luau, and you only hold a small stroke of the pen in all of those certifications. Roblox handles all of those things mostly internally, and you simply added to them. So you only hold the basic concepts of such things, which isn’t very useful. It would be far useful to work on a project on Unity, and have experience with Serdes, server validation techniques, and actual Net programming. You would be using known c# libraries, and the implementations of your networking system is unique to you. Roblox is a universal one, sadly! I personally write LaTex research papers on most of the projects I do for the LLC I work for and put those on my resume, since most of the stuff I do is focused on mathematics, physics, and engineering. It works perfectly fine and has really helped me!

posts.author: Velphion
posts.content: I definitely agree that portfolios and shipped projects would still matter the most. I just think certifications could still provide additional value, especially for younger developers who may not yet have years of professional experience, established resumes, or industry connections. Even outside of Roblox specifically, recruiters often like seeing some form of verified achievement, certification, or structured proof of learning rather than only self-claimed experience. For example, seeing a younger developer with verified certifications, active projects, and real experience can immediately make them appear more serious and committed compared to someone with only vague claims and no proof behind them. I don’t think certifications should replace portfolios, but more act as an additional credibility layer alongside actual work and experience.

posts.author: paswa
posts.content: Well to go along with this discussion. How would the validity of these certifications be verified? You speak of the younger generation. However to pair the validity of a certification, it requires someone’s identity to be linked to either their profile (having an ID attached to it), or to some internal database (username → identity). So automatically its restricted to people either 16+ or 18+.. If this wasn’t in place then you would lose the validity of the certifications.

posts.author: Velphion
posts.content: That’s honestly a very good point, and I do agree that verification and identity validation would probably be one of the biggest challenges with a system like this. Without some form of identity verification, certifications could easily lose credibility due to account sharing, purchased certifications, cheating, or people simply claiming someone else’s work as their own. That said, I don’t necessarily think every certification would need strict real-world identity verification to still provide value. Even platform-level verification tied to a long-term Roblox account history, production experience, or practical exams could still improve credibility compared to having no verification system at all. For more advanced/professional certifications though, I do agree that some level of identity verification would probably become necessary if Roblox wanted them to be taken seriously outside of the platform, especially on places like LinkedIn or resumes. And honestly, restrictions for higher-level certifications being limited to verified/older accounts would probably make sense if the goal is maintaining trust and legitimacy.

items.title: RPy — Python to Luau Compiler for Roblox

posts.author: kiritousd
posts.content: RPy — Python to Luau Compiler for Roblox Write Python. Ship to Roblox. RPy is a production-grade Python-to-Luau transpiler that combines the elegance and power of Python with native Luau performance. Compile, type-check, and sync your code directly into Roblox Studio while taking advantage of advanced compiler optimizations, SSA analysis, and full Roblox API awareness. Target Audience: Intermediate to Advanced Roblox developers (and engineering teams) who want to leverage the Python ecosystem, rigorous static analysis, and expressive syntax without sacrificing raw Luau VM performance. Compatibility: Fully compatible with Roblox Studio 2026+ and Luau versions supported by the Roblox client. Works for client, server, and replicated scripts. Overview RPy is not just a translator—it is a multi-stage compiler designed for production use. Python syntax is parsed, semantically analyzed, optimized, and output as idiomatic, high-performance Luau code. No heavy Python runtime is shipped; RPy maps Python semantics directly to Luau structures. Pipeline: Python AST → Lexical Scope Resolution → Middle-end IR → Dependency Graph → Optimization Passes → Target-specific Luau generation Key Features: SSA-based optimizer (Constant Folding, Dead Code Elimination, Peephole Optimization, Escape Analysis, SRA) Strict type mapping: Python type hints → Luau types --!strict headers for all modules Flow-sensitive semantic analysis Classes, inheritance, properties, static methods Lists, dictionaries, sets, comprehensions Module imports resolved to Roblox require() Minimal runtime shims for Python-only features Built-in code syncing with Roblox Studio via rpy watch Full Roblox API metadata baked into the compiler CLI tools ( rpy build , rpy watch , rpy doctor , rpy setup ) Custom VSCode extension with LSP features (diagnostics, completion, go-to-definition) Installation / Quick Start Get RPy up and running on your machine in under three minutes. Prerequisites Python 3.10+ — required for modern syntax and type hints Roblox Studio — target environment for compiled code Step-by-Step Setup Install the Transpiler pip install rpy-roblox Initialize Your Project rpy init my-awesome-game cd my-awesome-game Deploy the Studio Companion rpy install The companion plugin handles automatic syncing between your filesystem and Roblox Studio. Example: Advanced Object-Oriented Transpilation Python ( src/managers/PlayerManager.py ): from roblox import game, Instance from typing import List class PlayerManager: def __init__(self, start_pos: "Vector3"): self.start_pos = start_pos self.players: List["Instance"] = [] def spawn_hero(self, name: str) - "Instance": hero = Instance.new("Part") hero.Name = name hero.Position = self.start_pos hero.Parent = game.Workspace self.players.append(hero) return hero RPy Output ( .rpy/out/managers/PlayerManager.lua ): -- Generated by RPy — do not edit manually --!strict local PlayerManager = {} PlayerManager._method_types = {} PlayerManager.__index = PlayerManager

Total Build Time: 0.93 seconds Throughput: ~107.55 files per second benchmark_test 743×797 39.3 KB 2. primitive performance tests ( py_bool py_eq ) This tests the raw execution overhead of mapped Python primitives to see how fast the generated code executes. 1 Million Boolean/Modulo Checks: 0.0356 seconds 500,000 “Deep Equality” List Checks: 0.0254 seconds benchmark_test2 568×777 31.9 KB PC Specs: CPU: AMD Ryzen 7 5700G (3.8 GHz) GPU: AMD Radeon Graphics (integrated) RAM: 16 GB

items.title: Advanced Replay & Match Demo System

posts.author: de_ew
posts.content: Company Logo 348×132 4.74 KB Advanced Replay Match Demo System Developed by VIENNA Hey everyone! We’ve built an advanced, performance-focused Replay System designed specifically for high-frequency data tracking in Roblox experiences. Before we bring this to the Creator Store marketplace, we’d love to get your feedback on its features, UI flow, and overall design. System Layout: The Replay Browser User Interface (F4) built on the Seam framework What is it? Our system captures frame-by-frame gameplay and replicates it with pixel-perfect accuracy for replays. Engineered specifically for high-stakes competitive, FPS, and round-based titles, it uses a custom Server-Client split architecture to offload heavy calculations safely onto the server without sacrificing client FPS. First-Person Replication: Accurate raycast tracking and client input replication Core Features Architecture ReplayCodecV2: A custom compression codec sampling positions at 10Hz. It only records changes between frames, providing smooth playback with a minimal DataStore footprint. In-Game Browser Controls (F4): A fully animated, clean UI allowing players to search and filter replays by map or date. Includes standard media controls (Play/Pause, +/- 5s skipping, and playback speed multipliers). Advanced Spectating Input Overlay: Viewers can break into a fly-camera mode or spectate players with a built-in real-time Keystroke Overlay (displays WASD, Space, and mouse clicks). Tracked NPCs Entities: Seamlessly include custom NPCs in match recordings simply by applying Roblox’s CollectionService tags. Configurable Codebase: Full source-code access to exposed frontend modules, letting you freely modify configurations, visual themes (full dark mode support), custom Lucide icons, and sound effects. Views: Third-person viewpoint tracking, speed manipulation, and dynamic FOV cone projection What we’re looking for feedback on: UI/UX Layout: Does the exposed frontend layout look intuitive for a player or a moderator to navigate? Use Cases: What kind of experiences do you think would benefit most from a highly optimized D-compression replay system (e.g., fighting games, competitive racing, tactical shooters)? Integration Extensibility: Since the frontend and data-handling layers are fully exposed, how easy do you think it would be to build custom integrations on top of it (e.g., creating a CS2-style demo viewer, automated match highlights, or anti-cheat review tools)? 📊 POLL Help us gauge developer interest for the Creator Store launch by voting below. If you vote, please leave a quick comment telling us why or why not! Would you integrate this replay system into your game? Yes (This would be perfect for my project!) items.title: [Client Beta] Publish & Test Your Server Authoritative Experiences

posts.author: welblander
posts.content: Key Takeaways We are launching the Server Authority Client Beta today, so you can now publish for testing at scale. Stability and misprediction rates are much improved, and APIs are locked. Documentation and template projects with these latest changes are here . Please let us know about bugs and any feedback you have. Hi Creators, Today, we’re excited to move Server Authority from Studio Beta into Client Beta ! Client Beta means you can publish games leveraging Server Authority. This allows you to move beyond local testing so you can begin iterating with your development teams, QA staff, and community members in live environments. Pending issues you might find and some final polishing we’re already working on, we hope to fully launch in a few weeks. In the meantime, we recommend you publish privately or publish as an Experience Beta . UPDATE - May 8, 2026 - Check out @Khanovich ’s post here for a behind the scenes look on our progress so far! Understanding the “Client Beta” Phase First, thank you to everyone who tried Server Authority and provided feedback in Early Access and Studio Beta releases! You found some issues that we were able to fix and had some great input we were able to implement! But until now it wasn’t feasible to test with multiple concurrent players in production. Moving to Client Beta means you can now publish experiences using Server Authority! But this is still a Beta, so please keep a few things in mind: APIs Locked: APIs are now locked. That said, barring strong creator feedback, some APIs may change before General Availability based on your feedback. If this happens, you will have to make updates and republish. Mispredictions and Performance: We are working incredibly hard behind the scenes polishing mispredictions and performance issues. You should not need to make changes to your code to benefit from future improvements. Known Issues: Finally, there are still a few known bugs. These are discussed below. Because this is a Client Beta, your game will be playable by anyone, but they may experience unexpected issues. We recommend publishing your experience privately or as an “ Experience Beta ” to enable testing with controlled groups (QA, dedicated fans, etc) rather than releasing to a wide public audience. What’s New Since Our Last Update General Improvements We’ve been working hard since launching the Studio Beta. Mispredictions : Dramatically lowered mispredictions across a range of gameplay scenarios. Performance : Improved performance of rollback and resimulation. Studio UI : New UI in the Studio Workspace Properties for configuration of Server Authority prerequisites. These changes allow you to easily enable server authority and its prerequisites in a single section of the Workspace Properties widget. Instance Stitching : Client-side predictive Instance creation is now possible directly with Instance.new() . Instances created by the same script using BindToSimulation will be stitched together for a seamless experience

posts.author: Khanovich
posts.content: Shoomama: Are there still recommendations concerning the place size for server authority performance? I remember in a previous post, it was mentioned that server authority is only performant in experiences with 20-player servers or lower. Has this limit recommendation been raised? If so, what is the new recommended limit? I think you’ll have to play around with the kind of game you have. If you make a racing game that has server authoritative cars you may need to have it be 20 or lower, especially if collisions are enabled. If it’s just avatars, you can probably go higher. Server Authority increases CPU load on Clients and Servers, so consider your Server Authoritative games more demanding than usual. We’re working on something that should help with this on low end devices at the cost of input latency, but still thinking of an ideal solution. HealthyKarl: Is this fine or not fine? local function SetNetVar(varName, value) local con con = RunService:BindToSimulation(function() someHolder:SetAttribute(varName, value) con:Disconnect() end) end local syncedColor = Color3.fromHSV(math.fmod(time(), 1.0), 1.0, 1.0) SetNetVar("SyncedColor", syncedColor) If this is a crucial piece of data for Server Authority, you need this to happen on Client and Server. If the thing that is calling SetNetVar is happening outside of BindToSimulation you’re probably going to mispredict. BindToSimulation really need to be working with Control Loops for prediction and resimulation to work. paracosm daemon: I don’t think I should be slowly inched toward the SpawnLocation. This does not occur when everything but ServerAuthority mode is on, or when all of those are off, or etc. I feel like these cases should’ve been covered before either of these features were released to the client. I have to admit that these features feel like they were slightly rushed out. Shift-Lock isn’t a mode we are 100% supporting right now in SAuth, and this is why we are going into Client Beta. There is only so much a team of Roblox Engineers + QA Staff are going to find compared to the complexity of UGC that creators make. Combined with the fact that we are building new APIs that aren’t finished we think it’s incredibly valuable to get feedback from Devs making games without having to jump through Early Access channel hoops. Basically, we know there are going to be issues since we called out a bunch of Caveats, and we think we can resolve them better with Creator feedback starting now.

posts.author: Khanovich
posts.content: HealthyKarl: So the above is in BindToSimulation except that it disconnects right after. And I am wondering if it disconnecting right after, is fine or not. So, calling BindToSimulation doesn’t invoke any magical synchronization powers, so if you need this to happen consistently on Client and Server this won’t work I don’t think unless it’s triggered by something that synchronizes correctly for Server Authority. Basically, if SetNetVar is triggered by listening to an Input Changed event, since those are Synchronized to play at the right time on Client and Server, I think this should mostly work (but maybe not perfect). Ideally the BindToSimulation functions are long-lived loops that listen to synchronized state, because that’s how Server Authority was designed. paracosm daemon: You do not need shift lock to do this. Noted, I think I might know why this is happening. For Server Authority we had to quantize positional data on Physics Simulation outputs AND replication outputs so that we don’t lose precision over the network. I think this is having a slight “drift” behavior with the Avatar Controller. We will work on this ASAP. paracosm daemon: I think releasing this as a Studio beta should’ve been more-or-less that. This seems very rudimentary and I believe a lot of developers trying this feature out for the first time would be booting up a baseplate just to mess around with server authority using the default character controller, but as stated above, simple issues like this prevail. I think we’re getting into the realm of “what is the best way to roll out a product” territory (and I don’t know if there is truly a best way to do it but we do our best to figure out how to best serve the creator community). You’re right, I wish we would have caught and fixed these before Client Beta, in fact I wish we had 0 bugs when we let this go live. But based on the complexity of Roblox I don’t think this is/was possible. We were running internal playtests almost Daily (and sometimes on weekends) on real games we made/modified internally (shooters, racing games, soccer game). We could spend eternity polishing Server Authority without letting people publish games or test them on Roblox servers. That could in theory allow us to release an entirely perfect version of this at some unknown point in the future, but then the question becomes “is this useful for our community?” When Server Authority was in Studio Beta we had a few power-users that used it but in general it wasn’t very popular and we didn’t get a lot of feedback from a wide breadth of users. The only thing different now is that users don’t have to opt into the Studio Beta and can chose to opt in and test games on our Servers.

items.title: A Reminder About User Privacy and Third-Party Integrations

posts.author: Roblox
posts.content: Key Takeaways As a reminder, our Creator Third-Party App Policy outlines how individual user data can be handled outside of a game. Review our policy and Creator Terms to ensure user data is accessed securely and stays fully aligned with platform policies. Hi Creators, As outlined in our Data Stewardship Principles , we have a responsibility to protect user and creator data, ensuring Roblox remains a platform that players can trust. Creators may encounter third-party apps or SDKs that offer features which track Roblox players or broadcast their activity. While these integrations can power interesting concepts, using them in ways that expose individual player data can put a game in violation of Roblox’s Creator Terms and Creator Third-Party App Policy . To clear up any confusion and help developers navigate these tools safely, we’ve broken down exactly what is allowed and what to avoid. Our Policy To ensure player safety, protect minor privacy, and maintain user trust across the platform, we maintain strict guardrails regarding how individual user data can be handled outside of a game. Players must be able to trust that creators are using, storing, and protecting their data responsibly. When we refer to protected user information, this includes not just traditional personal data (like emails), but also platform-specific identifiers such as Roblox User IDs, usernames, display names, and individual player behavioral history. Creators may not leverage Roblox user data for the purposes of: Profiling and Tracking: Creators may not track Roblox users across third-party platforms or services, nor use player data to build, track, or enrich behavioral profiles about a user outside of Roblox. Unauthorized Data Sharing: Creators may not send individual user data to social platforms, chat applications, advertising networks, vendors, or partners for their own business purposes. Creators also may not sell, rent, lease, or syndicate this data. Bypassing Privacy Settings: Creators may not use player game data to surface live player activity or “rich presence” statuses on third-party platforms in a way that circumvents a user’s chosen Roblox account visibility or privacy settings. E xposing Data to Others: Creators may not build or release tools that expose one user’s individual data to another user. Examples of What to Avoid Sending user data to social platforms or chat applications Using player game data to surface live player activity or “rich presence” statuses on external platforms Circumventing Roblox account settings, including visibility and privacy preferences Analyzing or sharing a user’s friend list or past play history in a public or social environment How to Build Safely When creating features that require account data, OAuth 2.0 and our Open Cloud API are the official and safest approaches available. Using these ensures data is accessed securely and stays fully aligned with platform policies. To protect our community, information obtained through the

items.title: Running Successful Experiments: Insights from Top Creators

posts.author: OrogeneIstari
posts.content: TL;DR: Proven Results : Creators are seeing massive gains by running Experiments —such as a 50% increase in payer conversion or 12.5% higher playtime by changing variables like gameplay difficulty or testing different reward systems. Easy Setup : Experiments are easy to setup and work for every genre; create a config , choose what you want to test (e.g. game loop difficulty, rewards, shop prices), and start an experiment in Creator Hub . Start an Experiment Today: Check out our quick start guide below, watch our latest webinar , or read our documentation to learn how you can set up an experiment today. Hi Creators, It’s been 6 months since we launched Experiments , and we’re seeing more creators use it to de-risk updates and fine-tune core loops. By testing variables through Configs and Custom Matchmaking , you can move away from guesswork and let player data help guide development. Of the over one thousand experiments through the first quarter of this year, nearly 1 in 4 tests produced a statistically significant playtime lift, with an average detected positive increase of ~5%. This lets you: Optimize your experience: Test what has the most positive impact on important metrics like playtime, retention, and spend. Safely test new updates: Roll out features to a small % of your audience first to ensure stability before a full release. We caught up with a few creators to see how they’re using these tools. Here’s what they found: Build a Crazy Tower : A tower-builder centered around building the tallest tower possible using randomly selected blocks. The Outcome : They discovered a “sweet spot” with their game’s difficulty and had a 50% increase in payer conversion and a 10% boost in average session time The Experiment: The team tested different “block pools” to find the ideal difficulty level. They wanted to know if players preferred complex shapes that required strategy or simpler shapes that allowed for faster building. “Experiments are great! A/B testing is a great way to figure out what players actually want, while avoiding the emotional clutter / bias / human imperception that come from other methods.” - CrazyBellum, Build a Crazy Tower Trolls cannot break this tower : A series of classic Obbys, with unique twists that require players to work together (and not get trolled). The Outcome: Incentivizing players with rewards led to a 12.5% gain in playtime and a 5.3% boost in D1 Retention, which did not negatively impact monetization. The Experiment : To see if rewards drive engagement, the team tested offering a free item at the end of an Obby level. “Developer intuition and actual player behavior can vary dramatically. Adding free rewards doesn’t necessarily affect player spending if implemented right. I also learnt that very miniscule changes to game design can actually affect engagement a lot.” - MikkelDevs, Trolls cannot break this tower Weird Gun Game : A fresh take on the shooter genre, players get to customize their guns to take into battle

posts.author: Sametc4n
posts.content: This is solid guidance, but I’d add a few critical considerations from my experience running experiments across multiple games. The setup is straightforward, but your hypothesis matters more than the feature. Don’t just test things because they’re easy to change. Before launching any experiment, define:- What metric actually moves your needle — is it DAU, payer conversion, session length, or Day 7 retention? These often conflict, so be intentional.- Your sample size and duration — testing for 3 days with 500 players won’t catch weekly behavior shifts. Aim for at least 1-2 weeks with statistical significance (usually 5,000+ users per variant for reliable results).- Confounding variables — if you launch a difficulty experiment during a content update, you can’t isolate what actually moved retention.**For implementation specifics:**Use DataStores to track variant assignments so players remain consistent across sessions. Pair this with RemoteEvents to log granular behavior — don’t just measure final outcomes. Track intermediate steps: if difficulty changes affect progression funnels, you need to see where players drop off.I’ve seen creators confuse uplift with noise. A 12.5% playtime increase sounds great, but if your confidence interval is wide, you’re just lucky. Use tools that calculate statistical significance properly — I personally track this through custom dashboards, but Roblox’s built-in reporting is improving. One practical tip: Run experiments on non-monetized mechanics first (difficulty, progression pacing). Once you’re confident, then test monetization variables like shop prices or reward scheduling — the stakes are higher and player trust matters.The real win isn’t running experiments; it’s making them a repeatable process . Document what worked, why, and iterate from there.

items.title: Instance streaming best practices and techniques

posts.author: dear_gee
posts.content: Hi Creators! We just published a new guide covering some of the best practices and techniques for instance streaming, so you can more effectively use it in your experiences. Check out the Instance Streaming Techniques page! Below is a quick look at some of the existing streaming resources we’ve released along the way. A Look Back SLIM (Scalable Lightweight Interactive Models) is a system that works hand-in-hand with instance streaming to automatically generate optimized, lightweight representations of your models when they’re streamed out. Instead of models simply disappearing at a distance, SLIM composites them into efficient low-poly versions that preserve visual quality across all device types. Read more about SLIM in these posts: SLIM Beta and Introducing SLIM . This video explains ways to manage the engine’s rendering detail based on player distance from 3D objects in your experience. Also check out how instance streaming and other factors can affect the engine’s memory usage and performance: Server Authority is a networking model that is built on instance streaming to make the server the single source of truth for your experience’s state. This is particularly valuable for competitive games like first-person shooters, racers, and sports experiences, where client-side exploits like speed hacking or teleportation have historically been difficult to prevent. Read more about server authority in this post: Server Authority Beta As always, the core instance streaming doc is a great companion if you want to learn more about streaming. Check out the Instance Streaming Overview page. Let us know what you think We’d love to hear your feedback on this new guide. Let us know if we missed anything or if there are topics you’d like us to cover in future content. Drop a comment below and happy creating!
